Exercise 1.1 — Euclid's Division Algorithm
Chapter 1 — Real Numbers
Public Exam Prep
Telangana State Board · Chapter 1 · Real Numbers

Exercise 1.1 — Euclid’s Division Algorithm

HCF using Division Lemma · Proof-based questions using Division Algorithm

Theorem 1.1a = bq + r5 Questions
Book Formula — Theorem 1.1 (Division Algorithm)

a = bq + r   where 0 ≤ r < b

  • Step 1: Apply lemma to (c, d) → find q and r such that c = dq + r
  • Step 2: If r = 0, then d is HCF. If r ≠ 0, apply lemma to (d, r)
  • Step 3: Continue until remainder = 0. The divisor at that stage = HCF
Q1Use Euclid’s algorithm to find the HCF of
(i) 900 and 270    (ii) 196 and 38220    (iii) 1651 and 2032▼

Method: Divide larger by smaller. Write a = bq + r. Repeat with (b, r) until remainder = 0. The last divisor is the HCF.

(i) HCF of 900 and 270
Step 1: 900 = 270 × 3 + 90  [divide 900 by 270, quotient=3, remainder=90] Step 2: 270 = 90 × 3 + 0  [divide 270 by 90, remainder=0 → STOP]
✅HCF (900, 270) = 90

The remainder became 0 at Step 2. The divisor at that stage is 90. So HCF = 90.
Verify: 90 divides 900 (900÷90=10 ✓) and 90 divides 270 (270÷90=3 ✓)

(ii) HCF of 196 and 38220
Step 1: 38220 = 196 × 195 + 0  [remainder=0 in FIRST step itself → STOP]
✅HCF (196, 38220) = 196

When remainder = 0 in the very first step, the smaller number IS the HCF.
This means 196 divides 38220 exactly (38220 ÷ 196 = 195 ✓)

(iii) HCF of 1651 and 2032
Step 1: 2032 = 1651 × 1 + 381  [remainder=381] Step 2: 1651 = 381 × 4 + 127  [remainder=127] Step 3: 381 = 127 × 3 + 0  [remainder=0 → STOP]
✅HCF (1651, 2032) = 127

Took 3 steps. The divisor at Step 3 is 127. HCF = 127.
Verify: 127 × 13 = 1651 ✓ and 127 × 16 = 2032 ✓

🎯 Exam Tips
  • Always write the full equation a = bq + r for every step — don’t skip
  • Always start with the LARGER number as dividend (a)
  • HCF = the divisor at the step where remainder first becomes 0
  • Verify your HCF by checking it divides both original numbers
Q2Use division algorithm to show that any positive odd integer is of the form 6q + 1, or 6q + 3 or 6q + 5, where q is some integer.▼

Method: Let a be any positive integer and b = 6. Apply division algorithm. Get all possible forms. Identify which are odd.

Proof — Write Exactly Like This in Exam

Let a be any positive integer and b = 6.

By Division Algorithm: a = 6q + r, where 0 ≤ r < 6.

So r can be 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 or 5. This gives six possible forms:

a = 6q = 2(3q) → divisible by 2 → EVEN a = 6q + 1 = 2(3q) + 1 → not divisible by 2 → ODD ✓ a = 6q + 2 = 2(3q + 1) → divisible by 2 → EVEN a = 6q + 3 = 2(3q + 1) + 1 → not divisible by 2 → ODD ✓ a = 6q + 4 = 2(3q + 2) → divisible by 2 → EVEN a = 6q + 5 = 2(3q + 2) + 1 → not divisible by 2 → ODD ✓
✅Since 6q, 6q+2, 6q+4 are even, any positive odd integer must be of the form 6q+1, 6q+3, or 6q+5. Hence proved. ∎

Why we write 2(3q)+1 etc.: We factor out 2 to show clearly that 6q is even. Any number of the form 2k is even; 2k+1 is odd. This is the justification the examiner expects.

🎯 Exam Tips
  • Always show WHY each form is even — write 6q = 2(3q) to factor out 2
  • End with “Hence proved” and the ∎ symbol — examiners look for this
  • b = 6 gives 6 possible remainders: 0,1,2,3,4,5. Rule out even ones.
Q3Use division algorithm to show that the square of any positive integer is of the form 3p or 3p + 1.▼

Method: Let a be any positive integer, b = 3. By division algorithm r = 0, 1 or 2. Square each case and simplify.

Proof

Let a be any positive integer and b = 3.

By Division Algorithm: a = 3q + r, where r = 0, 1 or 2.

Case 1: a = 3q
a² = (3q)² = 9q² = 3(3q²) = 3p where p = 3q²
✅a² is of the form 3p
Case 2: a = 3q + 1
a² = (3q + 1)² = 9q² + 6q + 1 = 3(3q² + 2q) + 1 = 3p + 1 where p = 3q² + 2q
✅a² is of the form 3p + 1
Case 3: a = 3q + 2
a² = (3q + 2)² = 9q² + 12q + 4 = 9q² + 12q + 3 + 1  [write 4 as 3+1 to extract factor of 3] = 3(3q² + 4q + 1) + 1 = 3p + 1 where p = 3q² + 4q + 1
✅a² is of the form 3p + 1

In all three cases, a² is either 3p or 3p + 1. The form 3p + 2 never occurs. Hence proved. ∎

🎯 Exam Tips
  • Case 3 trick: write +4 as +3+1 so you can factor out 3 from the first three terms
  • Always write what p equals — e.g. “where p = 3q² + 2q”
  • Classic exam question: “Can square of any integer be of form 3p+2?” — Answer: NO!
Q4Use division algorithm to show that the cube of any positive integer is of the form 9m, 9m + 1 or 9m + 8.▼

Method: Let a be any positive integer, b = 3. r = 0, 1 or 2. Cube each case using (a+b)³ = a³ + 3a²b + 3ab² + b³.

Proof

Let a be any positive integer and b = 3.

By Division Algorithm: a = 3q + r, where r = 0, 1 or 2.

Case 1: a = 3q
a³ = (3q)³ = 27q³ = 9(3q³) = 9m where m = 3q³
✅a³ is of the form 9m
Case 2: a = 3q + 1
a³ = (3q + 1)³ = 27q³ + 27q² + 9q + 1 = 9(3q³ + 3q² + q) + 1 = 9m + 1 where m = 3q³ + 3q² + q
✅a³ is of the form 9m + 1
Case 3: a = 3q + 2
a³ = (3q + 2)³ = 27q³ + 3(9q²)(2) + 3(3q)(4) + 8 = 27q³ + 54q² + 36q + 8 = 9(3q³ + 6q² + 4q) + 8 = 9m + 8 where m = 3q³ + 6q² + 4q
✅a³ is of the form 9m + 8

The cube of any positive integer is always of the form 9m, 9m+1, or 9m+8. Hence proved. ∎

🎯 Exam Tips
  • Expand using (a+b)³ = a³ + 3a²b + 3ab² + b³
  • Answers jump from 1 to 8 — never write 9m+2, 9m+3, 9m+4 etc. Those are wrong!
  • Quick verify: 1³=1=9(0)+1, 2³=8=9(0)+8, 3³=27=9(3), 4³=64=9(7)+1 ✓
Q5Show that one and only one out of n, n + 2 or n + 4 is divisible by 3, where n is any positive integer.▼

Method: Let n = 3q + r where r = 0, 1 or 2. In each case check which of n, n+2, n+4 is divisible by 3. Show exactly one is.

Proof

Let n be any positive integer. By Division Algorithm with b = 3: n = 3q + r, where r = 0, 1 or 2.

Case 1: n = 3q
n = 3q → divisible by 3 ✓ n + 2 = 3q + 2 → NOT divisible by 3 n + 4 = 3q + 4 = 3(q+1) + 1 → NOT divisible by 3 Only n is divisible by 3.
✅Exactly one (n) is divisible by 3
Case 2: n = 3q + 1
n = 3q + 1 → NOT divisible by 3 n + 2 = 3q + 3 = 3(q+1) → divisible by 3 ✓ n + 4 = 3q + 5 = 3(q+1) + 2 → NOT divisible by 3 Only n+2 is divisible by 3.
✅Exactly one (n+2) is divisible by 3
Case 3: n = 3q + 2
n = 3q + 2 → NOT divisible by 3 n + 2 = 3q + 4 = 3(q+1) + 1 → NOT divisible by 3 n + 4 = 3q + 6 = 3(q+2) → divisible by 3 ✓ Only n+4 is divisible by 3.
✅Exactly one (n+4) is divisible by 3

In every case, exactly one of n, n+2, n+4 is divisible by 3 — never zero, never two. Hence proved. ∎

🎯 The Big Insight
  • n, n+2, n+4 cover all three remainder slots (0, 1, 2) when divided by 3
  • So exactly one of them always falls on a multiple of 3
  • “One and only one” proof: you must show AT LEAST one AND NOT MORE than one
Telangana State Board · Chapter 1 · Real Numbers

Exercise 1.2 — Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic

Prime Factorisation · HCF & LCM · Composite numbers · Last digit problems

Theorem 1.2HCF & LCM6 Questions
Golden Rules — Prime Factorisation Method (Theorem 1.2)
  • HCF = Product of LOWEST powers of COMMON prime factors
  • LCM = Product of HIGHEST powers of ALL prime factors
  • Key relation: HCF(a,b) × LCM(a,b) = a × b   (only for TWO numbers!)
Q1Express each of the following numbers as a product of its prime factors.
(i) 140    (ii) 156    (iii) 3825    (iv) 5005    (v) 7429▼

Method: Divide by the smallest prime (2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13...) repeatedly until quotient = 1. Write as product of primes.

(i) 140
140 ÷ 2 = 70 70 ÷ 2 = 35 35 ÷ 5 = 7 7 ÷ 7 = 1
✅140 = 2² × 5 × 7
(ii) 156
156 ÷ 2 = 78 78 ÷ 2 = 39 39 ÷ 3 = 13 13 ÷ 13 = 1
✅156 = 2² × 3 × 13
(iii) 3825
3825 ÷ 3 = 1275 1275 ÷ 3 = 425 425 ÷ 5 = 85 85 ÷ 5 = 17 17 ÷ 17 = 1 [Divisibility by 3 check: 3+8+2+5=18, divisible by 3 ✓]
✅3825 = 3² × 5² × 17
(iv) 5005
5005 ÷ 5 = 1001 1001 ÷ 7 = 143 143 ÷ 11 = 13 13 ÷ 13 = 1
✅5005 = 5 × 7 × 11 × 13
(v) 7429
7429 ÷ 17 = 437 437 ÷ 19 = 23 23 ÷ 23 = 1 [Not divisible by 2,3,5,7,11,13 → try 17: 7429÷17=437 ✓]
✅7429 = 17 × 19 × 23
🎯 Division Order & Divisibility Tips
  • Always try primes in order: 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19, 23...
  • Divisible by 2: ends in 0,2,4,6,8    By 3: digit sum divisible by 3
  • Divisible by 5: ends in 0 or 5    By 7,11,13: divide and check
  • Write final answer in index form: 2² × 5 × 7 not 2×2×5×7
Q2Find the LCM and HCF of the following integers by the prime factorization method.
(i) 12, 15 and 21    (ii) 17, 23, and 29    (iii) 8, 9 and 25    (iv) 72 and 108    (v) 306 and 657▼

Method: (1) Factorise each number. (2) HCF = lowest powers of COMMON primes only. (3) LCM = highest powers of ALL primes.

(i) 12, 15 and 21
12 = 2² × 3 15 = 3 × 5 21 = 3 × 7 Common prime: 3 (present in all three) HCF = 3¹ = 3 [only 3 is common to all, take lowest power = 3¹] LCM = 2² × 3 × 5 × 7 = 4 × 3 × 5 × 7 = 420 [all primes, highest powers]
✅HCF = 3    LCM = 420
(ii) 17, 23, and 29
17 = 17 (prime number) 23 = 23 (prime number) 29 = 29 (prime number) All three are distinct primes. No common factor between any pair. HCF = 1 LCM = 17 × 23 × 29 = 11339
✅HCF = 1    LCM = 11339

When all numbers are distinct primes, HCF is always 1 (no common factor).

(iii) 8, 9 and 25
8 = 2³ 9 = 3² 25 = 5² No prime is common to all three (different prime bases entirely). HCF = 1 LCM = 2³ × 3² × 5² = 8 × 9 × 25 = 1800
✅HCF = 1    LCM = 1800
(iv) 72 and 108
72 = 2³ × 3² 108 = 2² × 3³ Common primes: 2 and 3 (both present in each) HCF = 2² × 3² = 4 × 9 = 36 [lowest powers: min(3,2)=2, min(2,3)=2] LCM = 2³ × 3³ = 8 × 27 = 216 [highest powers: max(3,2)=3, max(2,3)=3] Verify: HCF × LCM = 36 × 216 = 7776 = 72 × 108 ✓
✅HCF = 36    LCM = 216
(v) 306 and 657
306 = 2 × 3² × 17 657 = 3² × 73 Common prime: 3 (present in both) HCF = 3² = 9 [lowest power of 3 is min(2,2)=2] LCM = 2 × 3² × 17 × 73 = 2 × 9 × 17 × 73 = 22338
✅HCF = 9    LCM = 22338
🎯 The Golden Rules — Never Confuse These!
  • HCF = LOWEST power × COMMON primes only (primes in ALL numbers)
  • LCM = HIGHEST power × ALL primes (even those in just one number)
  • HCF × LCM = a × b — works ONLY for two numbers, not three!
  • If all numbers are distinct primes → HCF = 1 always
Q3Check whether 6n can end with the digit 0 for any natural number n.▼

Method: For a number to end in 0, its prime factorisation must contain both 2 and 5. Show 6n can never have 5 as a factor.

For any number to end with digit 0, it must be divisible by 10. 10 = 2 × 5 So the prime factorisation must contain BOTH 2 AND 5. Now: 6n = (2 × 3)n = 2n × 3n The prime factorisation of 6n contains ONLY 2 and 3. The prime factor 5 is NEVER present in 6n. By the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic, prime factorisation is UNIQUE. So 5 can never appear in 6n for any natural number n.
✅6n can NEVER end with the digit 0 for any natural number n.

Key sentence for exam: Since 5 is not a factor of 6n = 2n × 3n, it cannot be divisible by 10, so it can never end in 0.

🎯 Exam Format — 3 Lines That Score Full Marks
  • Line 1: “For ending in 0, prime factorisation must contain both 2 and 5”
  • Line 2: “6n = 2n × 3n, which has no factor of 5”
  • Line 3: “By Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic, 6n can never end in 0”
  • Same logic applies for 4n, 8n, 15n type questions
Q4Explain why 7 × 11 × 13 + 13 and 7 × 6 × 5 × 4 × 3 × 2 × 1 + 5 are composite numbers.▼

Method: A composite number has factors other than 1 and itself. Show each expression = A × B where A, B > 1 by taking the last number as common factor.

(a) 7 × 11 × 13 + 13
= 13 × (7 × 11) + 13 × 1 [take 13 as common factor from both terms] = 13 × (7 × 11 + 1) = 13 × (77 + 1) = 13 × 78
✅= 13 × 78. Has factors 13 and 78, both > 1. Therefore it is a COMPOSITE number.
(b) 7 × 6 × 5 × 4 × 3 × 2 × 1 + 5
= 5 × (7 × 6 × 4 × 3 × 2 × 1) + 5 × 1 [take 5 as common factor] = 5 × (7 × 6 × 4 × 3 × 2 × 1 + 1) = 5 × (1008 + 1) = 5 × 1009
✅= 5 × 1009. Has factors 5 and 1009, both > 1. Therefore it is a COMPOSITE number.

Both numbers can be expressed as a product of two integers each greater than 1. So by definition both are composite.

🎯 Pattern to Spot Instantly
  • Look at the small number added at the end (+13, +5)
  • That number is ALWAYS a factor of one term in the product — pull it out!
  • Once you write A × B form, say: “has factors A and B both greater than 1, so composite”
Q5How will you show that (17 × 11 × 2) + (17 × 11 × 5) is a composite number? Explain.▼

Method: Take 17 × 11 as common factor from both terms. Express as a product. Show it has factors other than 1 and itself.

(17 × 11 × 2) + (17 × 11 × 5) = 17 × 11 × (2 + 5) [17 × 11 is common to BOTH terms] = 17 × 11 × 7 = 1309
✅1309 = 7 × 11 × 17. It has factors 7, 11 and 17 other than 1 and 1309. Therefore it is a COMPOSITE number.
🎯 Exam Tip
  • Take common factor out from BOTH terms. Express as product. Then explicitly state factors.
  • Write: “Since 1309 = 7 × 11 × 17, it has factors other than 1 and itself. So it is composite.”
Q6What is the last digit of 6100?▼

Method: Find the pattern of last digits of powers of 6. Observe if it repeats.

6¹ = 6 → last digit 6 6² = 36 → last digit 6 6³ = 216 → last digit 6 6&sup4; = 1296 → last digit 6 Pattern: every power of 6 always ends in 6. Reason: 6 × 6 = 36 (ends in 6), 36 × 6 = 216 (ends in 6)... Any number ending in 6, when multiplied by 6, ends in 6.
✅Last digit of 6100 = 6

The last digit of 6 raised to ANY natural number power is always 6.

🎯 Useful Pattern Facts
  • 6any always ends in 6
  • 5any always ends in 5
  • 1any always ends in 1
  • In exam: always show at least 3 examples before stating the conclusion
Telangana State Board · Chapter 1 · Real Numbers

Exercise 1.3 — Rational Numbers & Decimal Expansions

Theorem 1.3, 1.4, 1.5 · Terminating & Non-Terminating decimals · Converting repeating decimals

Theorem 1.3Theorem 1.4Theorem 1.54 Questions
Three Book Theorems — Know All Three!
  • Theorem 1.3: If decimal expansion terminates → q must be of the form 2n × 5m
  • Theorem 1.4: If q is of the form 2n × 5m → decimal terminates. To convert: multiply top and bottom to make denominator a power of 10.
  • Theorem 1.5: If q has any prime other than 2 or 5 → decimal is non-terminating repeating
Q1Write the following rational numbers in their decimal form and also state which are terminating and which are non-terminating, repeating decimal.
(i) 3/8    (ii) 229/400    (iii) 4⅕    (iv) 2/11    (v) 8/125▼

Method (Theorem 1.4): Check denominator. If only 2s and 5s → terminating. Multiply to make denominator a power of 10. Place decimal point. If other primes → non-terminating repeating.

(i) 3/8
Denominator: 8 = 2³ [only 2s → terminating] Need 5³ to make 10³: 3/2³ × 5³/5³ = (3 × 125)/(2³ × 5³) = 375/1000
✅3/8 = 0.375  —  Terminating decimal
(ii) 229/400
Denominator: 400 = 2&sup4; × 5² [only 2s and 5s → terminating] Need 5² more to balance (has 2&sup4; and 5², need 5&sup4; to make 10&sup4;): 229/(2&sup4;×5²) × 5²/5² = (229×25)/10000 = 5725/10000
✅229/400 = 0.5725  —  Terminating decimal
(iii) 4⅕ = 21/5
Denominator: 5 = 5¹ [only 5s → terminating] 21/5 × 2/2 = 42/10
✅4⅕ = 4.2  —  Terminating decimal
(iv) 2/11
Denominator: 11 [has prime 11, not just 2s and 5s → non-terminating] 2 ÷ 11 = 0.181818... [block ‘18’ repeats]
✅2/11 = 0.1̅8̅  —  Non-terminating, repeating decimal
(v) 8/125
Denominator: 125 = 5³ [only 5s → terminating] Need 2³ to make 10³: 8/5³ × 2³/2³ = (8 × 8)/(5³ × 2³) = 64/1000
✅8/125 = 0.064  —  Terminating decimal
🎯 Theorem 1.4 Step-by-Step
  • Count powers of 2 and 5 in denominator
  • If more 2s than 5s: multiply by missing 5s (top and bottom)
  • If more 5s than 2s: multiply by missing 2s (top and bottom)
  • Goal: make denominator = 10, 100, 1000... then shift decimal point
  • Always show the full working — don’t just write the decimal
Q2Without performing division, state whether the following rational numbers will have a terminating decimal form or a non-terminating, repeating decimal form.
(i) 13/3125   (ii) 11/12   (iii) 64/455   (iv) 15/1600   (v) 29/343   (vi) 23/23×52   (vii) 129/22×57×75   (viii) 9/15   (ix) 36/100   (x) 77/210▼

Method: (1) Simplify fraction to lowest terms. (2) Factorise denominator. (3) If only 2s and 5s → Terminating. Any other prime → Non-Terminating Repeating.

(i) 13/3125 3125 = 5&sup5; → only 5s ✓ → Terminating (ii) 11/12 12 = 2² × 3 → has prime 3 ✗ → Non-Terminating Repeating (iii) 64/455 455 = 5 × 7 × 13 → has primes 7 and 13 ✗ → Non-Terminating Repeating (iv) 15/1600 1600 = 2&sup6; × 5² → only 2s and 5s ✓ → Terminating (v) 29/343 343 = 7³ → has prime 7 ✗ → Non-Terminating Repeating (vi) 23/(2³×5²) Denominator = 2³×5² → only 2s and 5s ✓ → Terminating (vii) 129/(2²×5&sup7;×7&sup5;) Has 7&sup5; ✗ → Non-Terminating Repeating (viii) 9/15 Simplify FIRST: 9/15 = 3/5 Denominator = 5¹ → only 5 ✓ → Terminating (ix) 36/100 Simplify FIRST: 36/100 = 9/25 Denominator = 5² → only 5s ✓ → Terminating (x) 77/210 Simplify FIRST: 77/210 = 11/30 30 = 2 × 3 × 5 → has prime 3 ✗ → Non-Terminating Repeating
🎯 Most Important Exam Trap
  • For (viii), (ix), (x): ALWAYS simplify the fraction FIRST, THEN check denominator
  • Checking the unsimplified denominator can give wrong answer — common mistake!
  • Only check denominator after fraction is in lowest terms
Q3Write the following rationals in decimal form using Theorem 1.4.
(i) 13/25    (ii) 15/16    (iii) 23/(23.52)    (iv) 7218/(32.52)    (v) 143/110▼

Theorem 1.4 Method: Identify powers of 2 and 5 in denominator. Multiply both top and bottom by the missing factor to make denominator = 10n.

(i) 13/25
25 = 5² → missing 2² to make 10² = 100 13/5² × 2²/2² = 52/100
✅13/25 = 0.52
(ii) 15/16
16 = 2&sup4; → missing 5&sup4; to make 10&sup4; = 10000 15/2&sup4; × 5&sup4;/5&sup4; = (15 × 625)/10000 = 9375/10000
✅15/16 = 0.9375
(iii) 23/(23.52)
Denominator = 2³ × 5² To make 10³: need 5³, but only have 5² → multiply by 5/5 23/(2³×5²) × 5/5 = (23×5)/(2³×5³) = 115/1000
✅23/(23.52) = 0.115
(iv) 7218/(32.52)
Denominator has 3² — simplify numerator first: 7218 ÷ 9 = 802 [since 3² = 9 divides 7218] So 7218/(3²×5²) = 802/5² = 802/25 Now 25 = 5²: multiply by 2²/2²: 802/5² × 4/4 = 3208/100
✅7218/(32.52) = 32.08
(v) 143/110
110 = 2 × 5 × 11 → has prime 11 → Non-terminating 143 ÷ 110 = 1.3000... = 1.30̅
✅143/110 = 1.3̅0̅  —  Non-terminating repeating
🎯 Simple Rule for Theorem 1.4
  • Count 2s and 5s in denominator. Add the missing ones to BOTH top and bottom.
  • Goal: equal powers of 2 and 5 in denominator = power of 10
  • For (iv): when denominator has other primes like 3², simplify by dividing first
Q4Express the following decimals in the form of p/q, and write the prime factors of q. What do you observe?
(i) 43.123    (ii) 0.1201201    (iii) 43.1̅2̅    (iv) 0.6̅3̅▼

Method: Terminating decimals → write as fraction over power of 10. Repeating decimals → multiply by 10n (n = number of repeating digits), subtract original, solve.

(i) 43.123   [Terminating]
43.123 = 43123/1000 Denominator 1000 = 2³ × 5³ Prime factors of q: 2 and 5 only
✅p/q = 43123/1000    q = 2³ × 5³
(ii) 0.1201201   [Terminating]
0.1201201 = 1201201/10000000 Denominator 10000000 = 10&sup7; = 2&sup7; × 5&sup7; Prime factors of q: 2 and 5 only
✅p/q = 1201201/10000000    q = 2&sup7; × 5&sup7;
(iii) 43.12121212... = 43.1̅2̅   [Non-terminating repeating]
Let x = 43.121212... 100x = 4312.1212... [multiply by 100 since 2 digits repeat] Subtract: 100x - x = 4312.1212... - 43.1212... 99x = 4269 x = 4269/99 = 1423/33 Denominator 33 = 3 × 11 Prime factors of q: 3 and 11 (not just 2s and 5s!)
✅p/q = 1423/33    q = 3 × 11
(iv) 0.636363... = 0.6̅3̅   [Non-terminating repeating]
Let x = 0.636363... 100x = 63.6363... [multiply by 100 since 2 digits repeat] Subtract: 99x = 63 x = 63/99 = 7/11 Denominator 11 is a prime Prime factors of q: 11 (not 2 or 5!)
✅p/q = 7/11    q = 11
Key Observation — Write This in Your Exam!
  • For terminating decimals (i) and (ii): q has only 2 and 5 as prime factors (form 2n5m)
  • For non-terminating repeating (iii) and (iv): q has primes other than 2 and 5 (like 3, 11)
  • This confirms Theorems 1.3, 1.4 and 1.5 from our textbook
🎯 Converting Repeating Decimal to p/q
  • 1 digit repeats: multiply by 10, subtract → 9x = number
  • 2 digits repeat: multiply by 100, subtract → 99x = number
  • 3 digits repeat: multiply by 1000, subtract → 999x = number
  • Always simplify the final fraction to lowest terms!
Telangana State Board · Chapter 1 · Real Numbers

Exercise 1.4 — Irrational Numbers

Theorem 1.6 · Proof by Contradiction · Proving irrationality of expressions

Theorem 1.6Proof by Contradiction6 Questions
Book Method — Proof by Contradiction (used in every question)
  • Step 1: Assume the number IS rational → write as a/b where a,b are integers, b≠0, HCF(a,b)=1
  • Step 2: Rearrange algebraically to isolate the irrational part
  • Step 3: Show irrational part = rational → contradiction → original assumption was wrong → number is irrational
  • Theorem 1.6: If prime p divides a², then p divides a — state this by name in exam!
Q1(i)Prove that 1/√2 is irrational.▼

Strategy: If 1/√2 is rational, its reciprocal √2 would also be rational. But √2 is known to be irrational. Contradiction.

Proof by Contradiction

Assume 1/√2 is rational.

Then 1/√2 = a/b, where a, b are integers, b ≠ 0, HCF(a,b) = 1.

Rearranging: √2 = b/a

Since a and b are integers, b/a is rational. So √2 would be rational.

But this contradicts the known fact that √2 is irrational.

This contradiction arose from our assumption. So the assumption is false.

✅Therefore, 1/√2 is irrational. ∎
🎯 Exam Tip

For 1/√n type: if 1/√n is rational → its reciprocal √n is also rational → contradiction since √n is irrational. Short and direct!

Q1(ii)Prove that √3 + √5 is irrational.▼

Strategy: Assume rational, isolate √5, show it would be rational → contradiction.

Proof by Contradiction

Assume √3 + √5 is rational.

Let √3 + √5 = a/b, where a, b are co-prime integers, b ≠ 0.

Then √3 = a/b − √5.

Squaring both sides: 3 = a²/b² − 2(a/b)√5 + 5

Rearranging: 2(a/b)√5 = a²/b² + 5 − 3 = a²/b² + 2

So: √5 = (a² + 2b²)/(2ab)

Since a, b are integers, RHS is rational. So √5 would be rational.

But √5 is irrational. Contradiction!

✅Therefore, √3 + √5 is irrational. ∎
🎯 Exam Tip
  • Squaring isolates one surd. Then rearrange to show the other surd is rational.
  • Works for any sum of two surds: assume rational, square, isolate one → contradiction
Q1(iii)Prove that 6 + √2 is irrational.▼

Strategy: Assume rational, rearrange to isolate √2, show √2 would be rational → contradiction.

Proof by Contradiction

Assume 6 + √2 is rational.

Then 6 + √2 = a/b, where a, b are co-prime integers, b ≠ 0.

Rearranging: √2 = a/b − 6 = (a − 6b)/b

Since a and b are integers, (a − 6b)/b is rational. So √2 would be rational.

But √2 is irrational. Contradiction!

✅Therefore, 6 + √2 is irrational. ∎
🎯 Template for (rational + √prime) type
  • Assume a + √b = p/q → rearrange → √b = (p − aq)/q → rational → contradiction
  • Works for: 6+√2, 5−√3, 2+√5, 7−√11 etc.
Q1(iv)Prove that √5 is irrational.▼

Strategy (from book, same as √2 proof): Assume √5 = a/b (co-prime). Show 5 divides both a and b → contradicts co-prime.

Proof using Theorem 1.6

Assume √5 is rational.

Let √5 = a/b, where a, b are co-prime integers, b ≠ 0.

Squaring: 5 = a²/b²  →  5b² = a²

So 5 divides a². By Theorem 1.6 (if prime p divides a² then p divides a), 5 divides a.

Write a = 5c for some integer c.

Substituting: 5b² = (5c)² = 25c²  →  b² = 5c²

So 5 divides b². By Theorem 1.6 again, 5 divides b.

Now 5 divides both a and b. But a and b are co-prime. Contradiction!

✅Therefore, √5 is irrational. ∎
🎯 The √prime Template — Most Important Proof!
  • Assume √p = a/b (co-prime) → pb² = a² → p|a → a = pc → b² = pc² → p|b
  • Both a and b divisible by p → contradicts co-prime → irrational
  • Same proof works for √2, √3, √5, √7 — any prime!
  • ALWAYS mention “Theorem 1.6” by name — it earns marks!
Q1(v)Prove that 3 + 2√5 is irrational.▼

Strategy: Assume rational, rearrange to isolate √5, show √5 would be rational → contradiction.

Proof by Contradiction

Assume 3 + 2√5 is rational.

Then 3 + 2√5 = a/b, where a, b are co-prime integers, b ≠ 0.

So: 2√5 = a/b − 3 = (a − 3b)/b

Therefore: √5 = (a − 3b)/(2b)

Since a and b are integers, (a − 3b)/(2b) is rational. So √5 would be rational.

But √5 is irrational. Contradiction!

✅Therefore, 3 + 2√5 is irrational. ∎
🎯 Template for (a + b√c) type
  • Assume rational = p/q → isolate √5 → divide by coefficient → √5 = rational → contradiction
  • Works for: 3+2√5, 4−3√2, 5+7√3 etc.
Q2Prove that √p + √q is irrational, where p, q are primes.▼

Strategy (same as Example 10 in book): Assume rational, isolate √q, show it would be rational → contradiction since q is prime.

Proof by Contradiction (General Case)

Assume √p + √q is rational.

Let √p + √q = a/b, where a, b are co-prime integers, b ≠ 0.

Then √p = a/b − √q.

Squaring both sides: p = a²/b² − 2(a/b)√q + q

Rearranging: 2(a/b)√q = a²/b² + q − p

So: √q = (a² + (q−p)b²) / (2ab)

Since a, b are integers, RHS is rational. So √q would be rational.

But q is prime, so √q is irrational. Contradiction!

✅Therefore, √p + √q is irrational. ∎
🎯 Complete Strategy for ALL Ex 1.4 Proofs
  • Start: “Assume [expression] is rational”
  • Write: “Let it = a/b where a,b are co-prime integers, b ≠ 0”
  • Rearrange to isolate the irrational part on one side
  • Show that side = rational expression → contradiction
  • End: “This contradicts that √... is irrational. Therefore [expression] is irrational. ∎”
Telangana State Board · Chapter 1 · Real Numbers

Exercise 1.5 — Logarithms

Definition · Three Laws: Product, Quotient, Power · All 9 questions

Product RuleQuotient RulePower Rule9 Questions
Golden Conversion Formula (from your book)
loga N = x   ⇔   ax = N
This is the ONLY formula needed to start every log problem. Always convert to exponential form first!
① Product Rule
loga(xy) = logax + logay

Multiplication → Addition

② Quotient Rule
loga(x/y) = logax − logay

Division → Subtraction

③ Power Rule
loga xn = n ⋅ loga x

Exponent → Multiplier (pull down)

Special Values
loga 1 = 0    loga a = 1

a(loga N) = N

Q1Determine the value of the following.
(i) log255   (ii) log813   (iii) log2(1/16)   (iv) log71   (v) logxx   (vi) log2512   (vii) log100.01   (viii) log2/3(8/27)   (ix) 2(2+log23)▼

Method: Let logaN = x. Convert to exponential: ax = N. Express both sides as powers of the same base. Compare exponents.

(i) log255
Let log255 = x → 25x = 5 (5²)x = 5¹ → 52x = 5¹ Comparing powers: 2x = 1 → x = 1/2
✅log255 = 1/2
(ii) log813
Let log813 = x → 81x = 3 (34)x = 3¹ → 34x = 3¹ Comparing powers: 4x = 1 → x = 1/4
✅log813 = 1/4
(iii) log2(1/16)
Let log2(1/16) = x → 2x = 1/16 1/16 = 1/24 = 2−4 So 2x = 2−4 → x = −4 [Negative because N < 1 and base > 1]
✅log2(1/16) = −4
(iv) log71
Let log71 = x → 7x = 1 = 70 Comparing powers: x = 0 [log of 1 to ANY base = 0, because a0 = 1 always]
✅log71 = 0
(v) logxx
Let logxx = k → xk = x = x¹ Comparing powers: k = 1 [log of any number to ITSELF as base = 1, because a¹ = a always]
✅logxx = 1
(vi) log2512
Let log2512 = x → 2x = 512 512 = 29 (2,4,8,16,32,64,128,256,512) So 2x = 29 → x = 9
✅log2512 = 9
(vii) log100.01
Let log100.01 = x → 10x = 0.01 0.01 = 1/100 = 10−2 So 10x = 10−2 → x = −2
✅log100.01 = −2
(viii) log2/3(8/27)
Let log2/3(8/27) = x → (2/3)x = 8/27 8/27 = 2³/3³ = (2/3)³ So (2/3)x = (2/3)³ → x = 3
✅log2/3(8/27) = 3
(ix) 2(2+log23)
Use law: am+n = am × an 2(2+log23) = 22 × 2log23 Now use identity: alogaN = N So 2log23 = 3 Therefore: 4 × 3 = 12
✅2(2+log23) = 12
🎯 Q1 Master Tips
  • Let logaN = x → write ax = N → express same base → compare powers
  • log of 1 = 0 always. Log of base to itself = 1 always. Memorise these!
  • For (ix): use identity alogaN = N (log and exponential cancel out)
  • Fraction as N < 1 with base > 1 → answer is always negative
Q2Write the following expressions as log N and find their values.
(i) log 2 + log 5   (ii) log216 − log22   (iii) 3 log644   (iv) 2 log 3 − 3 log 2   (v) log 10 + 2 log 3 − log 2▼

Method: Step 1: Apply Power Rule first (bring coefficients up). Step 2: Apply Product Rule (combine + terms). Step 3: Apply Quotient Rule (handle − terms).

(i) log 2 + log 5
= log(2 × 5) [Product Rule: log x + log y = log xy] = log 10 = 1 [log1010 = 1 since 10¹ = 10]
✅log 2 + log 5 = log 10 = 1
(ii) log216 − log22
= log2(16/2) [Quotient Rule] = log28 = log22³ = 3 log22 [Power Rule] = 3 × 1 = 3 [log22 = 1]
✅log216 − log22 = log28 = 3
(iii) 3 log644
= log64(4³) [Power Rule: n log x = log xn] = log6464 = 1 [log of base to itself = 1]
✅3 log644 = log6464 = 1
(iv) 2 log 3 − 3 log 2
= log 3² − log 2³ [Power Rule on both terms] = log 9 − log 8 = log(9/8) [Quotient Rule]
✅2 log 3 − 3 log 2 = log(9/8)

9/8 is not a power of 10, so log(9/8) cannot be simplified to a whole number. This is the final answer.

(v) log 10 + 2 log 3 − log 2
= log 10 + log 3² − log 2 [Power Rule: 2 log 3 = log 9] = log 10 + log 9 − log 2 = log(10 × 9) − log 2 [Product Rule on first two] = log 90 − log 2 = log(90/2) = log 45 [Quotient Rule]
✅log 10 + 2 log 3 − log 2 = log 45
🎯 Order of Operations in Q2
  • Step 1: Power Rule FIRST — bring all number coefficients up as exponents
  • Step 2: Product Rule — combine all + terms into multiplication
  • Step 3: Quotient Rule — handle − terms as division
  • Always do + before − to avoid sign errors
Q3Evaluate each of the following in terms of x and y, if it is given that x = log23 and y = log25.
(i) log215   (ii) log27.5   (iii) log260   (iv) log26750▼

Method: Prime factorise each number into powers of 2, 3 and 5. Apply Product/Power rules. Replace log23 = x, log25 = y, log22 = 1.

(i) log215
15 = 3 × 5 log215 = log2(3×5) = log23 + log25 = x + y
✅log215 = x + y
(ii) log27.5
7.5 = 15/2 = (3 × 5)/2 log27.5 = log215 − log22 = (x+y) − 1 = x + y − 1 [since log22 = 1]
✅log27.5 = x + y − 1
(iii) log260
60 = 2² × 3 × 5 log260 = log22² + log23 + log25 = 2 log22 + x + y = 2(1) + x + y = 2 + x + y
✅log260 = 2 + x + y
(iv) log26750
6750 = 2 × 3375 = 2 × 3³ × 5³ [6750 ÷ 2 = 3375; 3375 = 3³ × 5³ = 27 × 125] log26750 = log22 + 3log23 + 3log25 = 1 + 3x + 3y
✅log26750 = 1 + 3x + 3y
🎯 Q3 Strategy
  • Prime factorise FIRST: break each number into powers of 2, 3, 5
  • Replace: log23 = x, log25 = y, log22 = 1
  • Use Product Rule to split products, Power Rule to bring exponents down
Q4Expand the following.
(i) log 1000   (ii) log(128/625)   (iii) log x2y3z4   (iv) log(p2q3/r4)   (v) log(x3/y2)▼

Method: Quotient Rule splits division into subtraction. Product Rule splits multiplication into addition. Power Rule brings exponents down as multipliers.

(i) log 1000
log 1000 = log 10³ = 3 log 10 = 3 × 1 = 3
✅log 1000 = 3 log 10 = 3
(ii) log(128/625)
128 = 2&sup7;, 625 = 5&sup4; log(128/625) = log 2&sup7; − log 5&sup4; = 7 log 2 − 4 log 5
✅log(128/625) = 7 log 2 − 4 log 5
(iii) log x²y³z4
= log x² + log y³ + log z4 [Product Rule] = 2 log x + 3 log y + 4 log z [Power Rule]
✅log x²y³z4 = 2 log x + 3 log y + 4 log z
(iv) log(p²q³/r4)
= log(p²q³) − log r4 [Quotient Rule: numerator minus denominator] = log p² + log q³ − log r4 [Product Rule on numerator] = 2 log p + 3 log q − 4 log r [Power Rule]
✅log(p²q³/r4) = 2 log p + 3 log q − 4 log r
(v) log(x³/y²)
= log x³ − log y² [Quotient Rule] = 3 log x − 2 log y [Power Rule]
✅log(x³/y²) = 3 log x − 2 log y
🎯 Expand Rule of Thumb
  • Numerator terms → positive (+) after Quotient Rule
  • Denominator terms → negative (−) after Quotient Rule
  • Then Power Rule: bring all exponents down as multipliers
Q5If x² + y² = 25xy, then prove that 2 log(x + y) = 3 log 3 + log x + log y.▼

Method: Manipulate the given equation to get a perfect square or product form. Then take log on both sides and apply the three laws.

Given: x² + y² = 25xy Add 2xy to both sides: x² + 2xy + y² = 27xy (x + y)² = 27xy Take log on both sides: log(x+y)² = log(27xy) 2 log(x+y) = log 27 + log x + log y [Power Rule, Product Rule] 2 log(x+y) = log 3³ + log x + log y 2 log(x+y) = 3 log 3 + log x + log y [Power Rule on log 3³]
✅Hence proved: 2 log(x+y) = 3 log 3 + log x + log y ∎
🎯 Proof Strategy
  • Key step: add 2xy to BOTH sides to get (x+y)² — that’s the insight
  • 27 = 3³ → log 27 = 3 log 3 (use Power Rule)
  • Always take log on both sides when you have a product or power
Q6If log((x+y)/3) = ½(log x + log y), then find the value of x/y + y/x.▼

Method: Simplify RHS using log laws. Equate the arguments. Expand and rearrange.

RHS: ½(log x + log y) = ½ log(xy) [Product Rule] = log(xy)1/2 [Power Rule] = log √(xy) So: log((x+y)/3) = log √(xy) Since logs are equal, arguments are equal: (x+y)/3 = √(xy) Squaring both sides: (x+y)²/9 = xy (x+y)² = 9xy x² + 2xy + y² = 9xy x² + y² = 7xy Divide both sides by xy: x/y + y/x = 7
✅x/y + y/x = 7
🎯 Key Steps to Remember
  • Simplify RHS to log √(xy), then equate with LHS argument: (x+y)/3 = √(xy)
  • Square both sides to remove the square root, then expand and rearrange
  • Dividing x²+y²=7xy by xy gives x/y + y/x directly
Q7If (2.3)x = (0.23)y = 1000, then find the value of 1/x − 1/y.▼

Method: Take log of each equation. Express log of each base. Use relationship between 2.3 and 0.23.

Given: (2.3)x = 1000 and (0.23)y = 1000 Taking log (base 10) of both: x ⋅ log(2.3) = log 1000 = 3 [so log(2.3) = 3/x] y ⋅ log(0.23) = 3 [so log(0.23) = 3/y] Now: 0.23 = 2.3/10 log(0.23) = log(2.3) − log 10 = log(2.3) − 1 [Quotient Rule] 3/y = 3/x − 1 Rearranging: 3/x − 3/y = 1 Dividing by 3: 1/x − 1/y = 1/3
✅1/x − 1/y = 1/3
🎯 Key Insight
  • 0.23 = 2.3/10, so log(0.23) = log(2.3) − 1. This links x and y.
  • Set up both equations, substitute the relationship, then rearrange for 1/x − 1/y
Q8If 2x+1 = 31−x then find the value of x.▼

Method: Bases are different (2 and 3) so we cannot compare exponents directly. Take log on both sides, apply Power Rule, collect x terms.

Take log on both sides: log 2(x+1) = log 3(1−x) (x+1) log 2 = (1−x) log 3 [Power Rule] x log 2 + log 2 = log 3 − x log 3 x log 2 + x log 3 = log 3 − log 2 x(log 2 + log 3) = log 3 − log 2 x = (log 3 − log 2)/(log 3 + log 2) x = log(3/2)/log(6) [Quotient & Product Rules]
✅x = (log 3 − log 2)/(log 2 + log 3) = log(3/2)/log 6
🎯 Key Technique
  • When bases are DIFFERENT, take log on BOTH sides — this is the only way
  • Apply Power Rule to bring exponents down as multipliers
  • Collect all x terms on one side, non-x terms on the other
Q9Is (i) log 2 rational or irrational? Justify your answer. (ii) log 100 rational or irrational? Justify your answer.▼
(i) Is log 2 rational or irrational?

Method: Assume rational, derive a contradiction using Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic.

Assume log102 = p/q (rational, p,q integers, q≠0, HCF(p,q)=1) Then: 10p/q = 2 Raise both sides to power q: 10p = 2q So: (2 × 5)p = 2q 2p × 5p = 2q For this equation to hold: 5p must equal 1 (since no 5 on RHS). This requires p = 0. But if p = 0, then 100 = 1 ≠ 2. Contradiction!
✅log 2 is IRRATIONAL.
(ii) Is log 100 rational or irrational?
log 100 = log10100 = log1010² = 2 log1010 [Power Rule] = 2 × 1 = 2 [since log1010 = 1] 2 is a rational number (= 2/1).
✅log 100 = 2 is RATIONAL.
🎯 Key Exam Fact
  • log of a POWER OF 10 (1, 10, 100, 1000, 0.1, 0.01...) → always rational (an integer!)
  • log of numbers that are NOT powers of 10 (like 2, 3, 5, 7, 20...) → irrational
  • This connects to Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic — unique prime factorisation
Telangana State Board · Chapter 2 · Sets

Exercise 2.1 — Introduction to Sets

Well-defined collections · Roster form · Set-builder form · Symbols & Subsets

Sets Basics 8 Questions
Key Definitions from Book
  • Set: A well-defined collection of distinct objects, written using { }
  • Element: If x is in set A, write x ∈ A (x belongs to A)
  • Not element: If x is NOT in A, write x ∉ A
  • Roster form: List all elements e.g. A = {1, 2, 3, 4}
  • Set-builder form: A = {x : x satisfies a condition}
  • Subset: A ⊆ B means every element of A is also in B
  • Null set: Empty set — written as φ or { }. Has no elements. n(φ) = 0
Q1Which of the following are sets? Justify your answer.
(i) The collection of all the months of a year beginning with the letter “J”.
(ii) The collection of ten most talented writers of India.
(iii) A team of eleven best cricket batsmen of the world.
(iv) The collection of all boys in your class.
(v) The collection of all even integers.▼
Book Rule: What makes a SET?

A set must be well-defined — we can clearly decide whether any given object belongs or not. If the answer depends on personal opinion, it is NOT a set.

(i) The collection of all months beginning with “J”
Months starting with J: January, June, July. We can clearly say which months qualify. No ambiguity. Collection = {January, June, July}
✅YES — it IS a set. It is well-defined because we can clearly determine which months belong.
(ii) The collection of ten most talented writers of India
The word "most talented" is subjective. Person A may choose different writers than Person B. We CANNOT clearly say who belongs and who does not.
❌NOT a set. “Most talented” depends on personal opinion — it is not well-defined.
(iii) A team of eleven best cricket batsmen of the world
The word "best" is a matter of opinion. Different selectors will pick different players. No unique answer.
❌NOT a set. “Best” is subjective. Different people have different opinions.
(iv) The collection of all boys in your class
All boys in the class are clearly known from the register. Any student is either a boy in the class or is not.
✅YES — it IS a set. It is well-defined. We can clearly say who belongs.
(v) The collection of all even integers
For any number: is it divisible by 2? Yes = even = belongs. No = does not belong. Clear rule, no ambiguity. Set = {..., -4, -2, 0, 2, 4, 6, ...}
✅YES — it IS a set. The rule “divisible by 2” clearly identifies every member.
🎯 Exam Tip — How to Justify in 1 Line
  • For a SET: write “It is well-defined because we can clearly determine whether any object belongs to it or not.”
  • For NOT a SET: write “It is not well-defined because the word ‘____’ is subjective and depends on opinion.”
  • Opinion words that make it NOT a set: best, most, talented, beautiful, good, favourite
Q2If A = {0, 2, 4, 6}, B = {3, 5, 7} and C = {p, q, r}, then fill in the appropriate symbol ∈ or ∉ in the blanks.
(i) 0 ..... A    (ii) 3 ..... C    (iii) 4 ..... B    (iv) 8 ..... A    (v) p ..... C    (vi) 7 ..... B▼

Method: Check if the element appears in the set. If YES → use ∈. If NO → use ∉.

(i) 0 ..... A → Is 0 in A={0,2,4,6}? YES → 0 ∈ A (ii) 3 ..... C → Is 3 in C={p,q,r}? NO → 3 ∉ C (iii)4 ..... B → Is 4 in B={3,5,7}? NO → 4 ∉ B (iv) 8 ..... A → Is 8 in A={0,2,4,6}? NO → 8 ∉ A (v) p ..... C → Is p in C={p,q,r}? YES → p ∈ C (vi) 7 ..... B → Is 7 in B={3,5,7}? YES → 7 ∈ B
🎯 Remember
  • ∈ = “belongs to” — element IS in the set
  • ∉ = “does not belong to” — element is NOT in the set
Q3Express the following statements using symbols.
(i) The element ‘x’ does not belong to ‘A’.
(ii) ‘d’ is an element of the set ‘B’.
(iii) ‘1’ belongs to the set of Natural numbers.
(iv) ‘8’ does not belong to the set of prime numbers P.▼

Translate English to symbols: “belongs to” = ∈    “does not belong to” = ∉

(i) “x does not belong to A” → x ∉ A (ii) “d is an element of B” → d ∈ B (iii)“1 belongs to Natural numbers N” → 1 ∈ N (iv) “8 does not belong to primes P” Because 8 = 2 x 4, it is composite, not prime. → 8 ∉ P
🎯 Exam Tip

This is usually a 1-mark question. Write the symbol clearly and correctly. ∈ and ∉ must be distinguishable!

Q4State whether the following statements are true or false. Justify your answer.
(i) 5 ∉ set of prime numbers    (ii) S = {5, 6, 7} implies 8 ∈ S.
(iii) −5 ∉ W where ‘W’ is the set of whole numbers.
(iv) 8/11 ∈ ℤ where ‘ℤ’ is the set of integers.▼
(i) 5 ∉ set of prime numbers
A prime number has exactly two factors: 1 and itself. 5 has factors: 1 and 5 only. So 5 IS a prime number. Therefore 5 ∈ {prime numbers}, not ∉.
❌FALSE. 5 is a prime number. Correct symbol: 5 ∈ {prime numbers}.
(ii) S = {5, 6, 7} implies 8 ∈ S
S = {5, 6, 7}. The only elements are 5, 6 and 7. 8 is not listed in S. So 8 ∉ S.
❌FALSE. 8 is not in S = {5, 6, 7}. Correct: 8 ∉ S.
(iii) −5 ∉ W where W = whole numbers
Whole numbers W = {0, 1, 2, 3, 4, ...} Whole numbers are non-negative only. −5 is negative. So −5 is NOT in W.
✅TRUE. Whole numbers start from 0. Negative numbers like −5 are not whole numbers.
(iv) 8/11 ∈ ℤ where ℤ = integers
Integers ℤ = {..., −2, −1, 0, 1, 2, 3, ...} — whole numbers only, no fractions. 8/11 is a fraction. It is NOT an integer. So 8/11 ∉ ℤ.
❌FALSE. 8/11 is a fraction, not an integer. Correct: 8/11 ∉ ℤ. (It belongs to ℚ, the rationals.)
🎯 Number Sets — Must Know!
  • N = Natural = {1,2,3,...}   W = Whole = {0,1,2,...}   ℤ = Integers = {...,−1,0,1,...}
  • ℚ = Rational = all p/q fractions   ℝ = Real = all numbers
  • Order: N ⊂ W ⊂ ℤ ⊂ ℚ ⊂ ℝ
Q5Write the following sets in roster form.
(i) B = {x : x is a natural number smaller than 6}
(ii) C = {x : x is a two-digit natural number such that the sum of its digits is 8}
(iii) D = {x : x is a prime number which is a divisor of 60}
(iv) E = {x : x is an alphabet in BETTER}▼

Method: Read the condition. Find all values that satisfy it. List inside { }. Write each element only ONCE.

(i) B = {x : x is a natural number smaller than 6}
Natural numbers smaller than 6: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 ("smaller than 6" means up to 5, not including 6)
✅B = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}
(ii) C = {x : two-digit number, sum of digits = 8}
Go through systematically: 17(1+7=8), 26(2+6=8), 35(3+5=8), 44(4+4=8), 53(5+3=8), 62(6+2=8), 71(7+1=8), 80(8+0=8)
✅C = {17, 26, 35, 44, 53, 62, 71, 80}
(iii) D = {x : prime number which is a divisor of 60}
Factorise 60: 60 = 2 x 2 x 3 x 5 = 2² x 3 x 5 Prime factors of 60 are: 2, 3, 5
✅D = {2, 3, 5}
(iv) E = {x : alphabet in BETTER}
Letters in BETTER: B, E, T, T, E, R In a set, no repetition. Distinct letters: B, E, T, R
✅E = {B, E, T, R}
🎯 Exam Tips
  • Never repeat elements in roster form. {B,E,T,R} not {B,E,T,T,E,R}
  • For digit sum: be systematic, start from 17 and go to 80
  • Prime divisors = prime factors of the number
Q6Write the following sets in the set-builder form.
(i) {3, 6, 9, 12}    (ii) {2, 4, 8, 16, 32}    (iii) {5, 25, 125, 625}    (iv) {1, 4, 9, 16, 25, … 100}▼

Method: Find the pattern in the elements. Write: {x : rule on x, range of x}.

(i) {3, 6, 9, 12}
3=3x1, 6=3x2, 9=3x3, 12=3x4 → multiples of 3, up to 4x3
✅{x : x is a multiple of 3, x ≤ 12, x ∈ N}
(ii) {2, 4, 8, 16, 32}
2=2¹, 4=2², 8=2³, 16=2&sup4;, 32=2&sup5; → powers of 2
✅{x : x = 2ⁿ, n ∈ N, n ≤ 5}
(iii) {5, 25, 125, 625}
5=5¹, 25=5², 125=5³, 625=5&sup4; → powers of 5
✅{x : x = 5ⁿ, n ∈ N, n ≤ 4}
(iv) {1, 4, 9, 16, 25, … 100}
1=1², 4=2², 9=3², …, 100=10² → perfect squares from 1 to 10
✅{x : x = n², n ∈ N, n ≤ 10}
🎯 Pattern Tips
  • Doubling elements (2,4,8,16) → powers of 2
  • Arithmetic pattern (3,6,9,12) → multiples
  • 1,4,9,16,25 → always perfect squares (n²)
Q7Write the following sets in roster form.
(i) A = {x : x is a natural number greater than 50 but smaller than 100}
(ii) B = {x : x is an integer, x² = 4}
(iii) D = {x : x is a letter in the word “LOYAL”}▼
(i) A = natural number, 50 < x < 100
Natural numbers strictly between 50 and 100: 51 to 99 (50 and 100 are excluded by "greater than" and "smaller than")
✅A = {51, 52, 53, …, 99}
(ii) B = integer with x² = 4
x² = 4 → x = +2 or x = −2 Both +2 and −2 are integers, so both are included.
✅B = {−2, 2}

Note: If domain was “natural number”, only {2} since −2 is not natural.

(iii) D = letters in “LOYAL”
Letters in LOYAL: L, O, Y, A, L L appears twice — but sets have no repetition. Distinct letters: L, O, Y, A
✅D = {L, O, Y, A}
🎯 Common Mistakes
  • For x²=4: don’t forget negative! x = ±2. Include both if domain allows.
  • For word-letters: write each letter once only, however many times it repeats
Q8Match the roster form with set builder form.
(i) {1, 2, 3, 6}    (ii) {2, 3}    (iii) {m, a, t, h, e, i, c, s}    (iv) {1, 3, 5, 7, 9}
(a) {x : x is prime number and a divisor of 6}    (b) {x : x is an odd natural number smaller than 10}
(c) {x : x is a natural number and divisor of 6}    (d) {x : x is a letter of the word MATHEMATICS}▼

Method: For each roster set, identify the property that ALL elements share. Match with the set-builder that describes that property.

(i) {1, 2, 3, 6}
What do 1,2,3,6 have in common? They are ALL the natural number divisors of 6: 6÷1=6, 6÷2=3, 6÷3=2, 6÷6=1
✅Matches (c): {x : x is a natural number and divisor of 6}
(ii) {2, 3}
2 and 3 are both PRIME and both DIVISORS of 6 (since 6=2x3). These are the prime divisors of 6 only.
✅Matches (a): {x : x is prime number and a divisor of 6}
(iii) {m, a, t, h, e, i, c, s}
MATHEMATICS → M,A,T,H,E,M,A,T,I,C,S → distinct: m,a,t,h,e,i,c,s
✅Matches (d): {x : x is a letter of the word MATHEMATICS}
(iv) {1, 3, 5, 7, 9}
These are the odd natural numbers less than 10: 1,3,5,7,9
✅Matches (b): {x : x is an odd natural number smaller than 10}
🎯 Matching Strategy
  • Count elements first — it helps narrow options
  • Find the shared property of all elements
  • Verify: the set-builder must produce EXACTLY the roster elements, no more, no less
Telangana State Board · Chapter 2 · Sets

Exercise 2.2 — Operations on Sets

Union (∪) · Intersection (∩) · Difference (A−B) · Disjoint sets

Union Intersection 7 Questions
Three Set Operations
  • Union A ∪ B: ALL elements in A OR B (or both) — no duplicates
  • Intersection A ∩ B: ONLY elements in BOTH A AND B
  • Difference A − B: Elements in A but NOT in B
  • Disjoint: A ∩ B = φ (no common elements at all)
Q1If A = {1, 2, 3, 4} and B = {1, 2, 3, 5, 6}, then find A ∩ B and B ∩ A. Are they equal?▼

Method for Intersection: Go through elements one by one. Pick only those present in BOTH sets.

A = {1,2,3,4} and B = {1,2,3,5,6} Check each element of A — is it also in B? 1 → in B? YES 2 → in B? YES 3 → in B? YES 4 → in B? NO A ∩ B = {1, 2, 3} Check each element of B — is it also in A? 1 → in A? YES 2 → in A? YES 3 → in A? YES 5 → NO 6 → NO B ∩ A = {1, 2, 3}
✅A ∩ B = B ∩ A = {1, 2, 3}. Yes, they are equal.

Conclusion: Intersection is commutative — A ∩ B = B ∩ A always. The order does not matter.

🎯 Exam Tip

Always write the conclusion: “A ∩ B = B ∩ A, so intersection is commutative.” — this earns the explanation mark!

Q2If A = {0, 2, 4}, find A ∩ φ and A ∩ A. Comment.▼
Finding A ∩ φ
A = {0,2,4} and φ = {} (empty — no elements at all) Intersection = elements common to BOTH. φ has NO elements, so there are NO common elements. A ∩ φ = φ
✅A ∩ φ = φ
Finding A ∩ A
A = {0,2,4} intersected with the same A = {0,2,4} Every element of A is in A. All are “common”. A ∩ A = {0,2,4} = A
✅A ∩ A = A

Comment: These results hold for ANY set A:
(1) A ∩ φ = φ    (2) A ∩ A = A

🎯 Exam Tip

The question says “comment” — always write both results as general statements, not just for this specific A.

Q3If A = {2, 4, 6, 8, 10} and B = {3, 6, 9, 12, 15}, find A − B and B − A.▼

Method for A − B: Start with all elements of A. Remove any element that also appears in B.

Finding A − B
A = {2,4,6,8,10} B = {3,6,9,12,15} Common element (A ∩ B) = {6} Remove 6 from A: A − B = {2, 4, 8, 10}
✅A − B = {2, 4, 8, 10}
Finding B − A
Remove common element 6 from B: B − A = {3, 9, 12, 15}
✅B − A = {3, 9, 12, 15}

A − B ≠ B − A. Order matters in set difference!

🎯 Exam Tips
  • Find A ∩ B first — that is what gets removed from each side
  • A−B: remove shared elements from A    B−A: remove shared elements from B
Q4If A and B are two sets such that A ⊆ B, then what is A ∪ B?▼

A ⊆ B means every element of A is already in B. When we take union, A adds nothing new.

Given: A ⊆ B (every element of A is also in B) Union A ∪ B = all elements in A OR in B. Since every element of A is already in B, A ∪ B = B (A contributes nothing new)
✅If A ⊆ B, then A ∪ B = B

Example: A={2,4}, B={2,4,6,8}. A⊆B. So A∪B={2,4,6,8}=B. ✓
Also note: A⊆B → A∩B = A (the smaller set is the intersection)

🎯 Key Results to Remember
  • If A⊆B → A∪B = B (the bigger set B contains everything)
  • If A⊆B → A∩B = A (the smaller set A is the intersection)
  • These two results go together — learn them as a pair!
Q5Let A = {x : x is a natural number}, B = {x : x is an even natural number}, C = {x : x is an odd natural number} and D = {x : x is a prime number}.
Find A ∩ B, A ∩ C, A ∩ D, B ∩ C, B ∩ D and C ∩ D.▼

A = all naturals, B = even naturals, C = odd naturals, D = primes. Note: B, C, D are all subsets of A.

A ∩ B and A ∩ C and A ∩ D
B, C, and D are all subsets of A. When a subset intersects with its superset, the result is the subset itself. A ∩ B = B = {2,4,6,8,...} (even natural numbers) A ∩ C = C = {1,3,5,7,...} (odd natural numbers) A ∩ D = D = {2,3,5,7,...} (prime numbers)
B ∩ C
B = even numbers, C = odd numbers. A number CANNOT be both even and odd simultaneously. No common elements exist.
✅B ∩ C = φ (B and C are disjoint)
B ∩ D
We need numbers that are BOTH even AND prime. 2 is even (divisible by 2) AND prime (only factors: 1 and 2). All other even numbers (4,6,8,...) are divisible by 2 and something else → NOT prime. So only 2 qualifies.
✅B ∩ D = {2} — 2 is the ONLY even prime number!
C ∩ D
We need numbers that are BOTH odd AND prime. 3,5,7,11,13,17,... are all odd primes. (2 is prime but even, so excluded here)
✅C ∩ D = {3, 5, 7, 11, 13, ...} = set of all odd primes
🎯 Board Exam Favourites from This Question
  • 2 is the ONLY even prime number — appears in many exams!
  • Even ∩ Odd = φ — they are always disjoint
  • If A ⊆ B → A ∩ B = A (the smaller set)
Q6If A = {3, 6, 9, 12, 15, 18, 21}, B = {4, 8, 12, 16, 20}, C = {2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 16} and D = {5, 10, 15, 20}, find:
(i) A−B   (ii) A−C   (iii) A−D   (iv) B−A   (v) C−A   (vi) D−A   (vii) B−C   (viii) B−D   (ix) C−B   (x) D−B▼

Strategy: Find common elements between each pair first. Then remove them from the appropriate side.

A={3,6,9,12,15,18,21} B={4,8,12,16,20} C={2,4,6,8,10,12,14,16} D={5,10,15,20} Common elements between pairs: A∩B={12} A∩C={6,12} A∩D={15} B∩C={4,8,12,16} B∩D={20} (i) A−B = Remove {12} from A = {3,6,9,15,18,21} (ii) A−C = Remove {6,12} from A = {3,9,15,18,21} (iii) A−D = Remove {15} from A = {3,6,9,12,18,21} (iv) B−A = Remove {12} from B = {4,8,16,20} (v) C−A = Remove {6,12} from C = {2,4,8,10,14,16} (vi) D−A = Remove {15} from D = {5,10,20} (vii) B−C = Remove {4,8,12,16} from B = {20} (viii)B−D = Remove {20} from B = {4,8,12,16} (ix) C−B = Remove {4,8,12,16} from C = {2,6,10,14} (x) D−B = Remove {20} from D = {5,10,15}
🎯 Exam Tip for Large Questions
  • Find the intersection of each pair first — that is what gets removed
  • Work systematically to avoid mistakes with so many parts
Q7State whether each of the following statements is true or false. Justify your answers.
(i) {2, 3, 4, 5} and {3, 6} are disjoint sets.
(ii) {a, e, i, o, u} and {a, b, c, d} are disjoint sets.
(iii) {2, 6, 10, 14} and {3, 7, 11, 15} are disjoint sets.
(iv) {2, 6, 10} and {3, 7, 11} are disjoint sets.▼

Two sets are disjoint if A ∩ B = φ. Find the intersection. If any common element exists → NOT disjoint (FALSE). If none → disjoint (TRUE).

(i) {2,3,4,5} and {3,6}
{2,3,4,5} ∩ {3,6} = {3} (3 is in BOTH sets) Since ∩ ≠ φ, they are NOT disjoint.
❌FALSE. 3 is a common element. These sets are NOT disjoint.
(ii) {a,e,i,o,u} and {a,b,c,d}
{a,e,i,o,u} ∩ {a,b,c,d} = {a} (‘a’ is in BOTH sets) Since ∩ ≠ φ, they are NOT disjoint.
❌FALSE. ‘a’ is common to both. NOT disjoint.
(iii) {2,6,10,14} and {3,7,11,15}
Check each element of first set against second: 2 in second? NO. 6? NO. 10? NO. 14? NO. ∩ = φ. No common elements.
✅TRUE. No common elements. These are disjoint sets.
(iv) {2,6,10} and {3,7,11}
2 in second? NO. 6? NO. 10? NO. ∩ = φ. No common elements.
✅TRUE. No common elements. These are disjoint sets.
🎯 Exam Format
  • Always show the intersection calculation: write “A ∩ B = {element}” or “A ∩ B = φ”
  • Then state TRUE or FALSE and give the reason
Telangana State Board · Chapter 2 · Sets

Exercise 2.3 — Equal Sets & Subsets

Equal sets · Subsets · Comparing sets · Listing all subsets

Equal Sets 5 Questions
Key Definitions
  • Equal sets: A = B means A ⊆ B AND B ⊆ A (same elements, any order)
  • Subset: A ⊆ B means every element of A is also in B
  • φ is subset of every set: φ ⊆ A for any set A
  • Every set is subset of itself: A ⊆ A
  • Number of subsets: A set with n elements has 2ⁿ subsets
Q1Which of the following sets are equal?
A = {x : x is a letter in the word FOLLOW}, B = {x : x is a letter in the word FLOW} and C = {x : x is a letter in the word WOLF}▼

Convert each to roster form (remove repeated letters). Then compare. Same elements = equal sets.

A = letters in FOLLOW: F,O,L,L,O,W → remove repeats → A = {F,O,L,W} B = letters in FLOW: F,L,O,W → all distinct → B = {F,L,O,W} C = letters in WOLF: W,O,L,F → all distinct → C = {W,O,L,F} Compare: A = {F,O,L,W} B = {F,L,O,W} C = {W,O,L,F} All three contain EXACTLY the same elements: F, L, O, W (Order does not matter in sets!) A ⊆ B and B ⊆ A → A = B A ⊆ C and C ⊆ A → A = C
✅A = B = C. All three sets are equal.
🎯 Exam Tip
  • To prove two sets equal: show A ⊆ B AND B ⊆ A
  • In roster form: order does not matter. {1,2,3} = {3,1,2}
  • Always remove repeated elements before comparing
Q2Consider the following sets and fill up the blanks with = or ≠ so as to make the statement true.
A = {1, 2, 3}   B = {The first three natural numbers}   C = {a, b, c, d}   D = {d, c, a, b}   E = {a, e, i, o, u}   F = {set of vowels in English Alphabet}
(i) A .... B   (ii) A .... E   (iii) C .... D   (iv) D .... F   (v) F .... A   (vi) D .... E   (vii) F .... B▼

First convert all descriptions to roster form. Then compare element by element.

A = {1,2,3} B = first three natural numbers = {1,2,3} C = {a,b,c,d} D = {d,c,a,b} = {a,b,c,d} [rearranged] E = {a,e,i,o,u} F = vowels in English = {a,e,i,o,u} (i) A={1,2,3} vs B={1,2,3} → Same elements → A = B (ii) A={1,2,3} vs E={a,e,i,o,u} → Different → A ≠ E (iii) C={a,b,c,d} vs D={a,b,c,d} → Same elements → C = D (iv) D={a,b,c,d} vs F={a,e,i,o,u} → b,c,d vs e,i,o → D ≠ F (v) F={a,e,i,o,u} vs A={1,2,3} → Completely different → F ≠ A (vi) D={a,b,c,d} vs E={a,e,i,o,u} → Different → D ≠ E (vii) F={a,e,i,o,u} vs B={1,2,3} → Completely different → F ≠ B
🎯 Exam Tip

Always convert set-builder/description to roster form first. “First three natural numbers” = {1,2,3} not {0,1,2}!

Q3In each of the following, state whether A = B or not.
(i) A = {a,b,c,d}   B = {d,c,a,b}
(ii) A = {4,8,12,16}   B = {8,4,16,18}
(iii) A = {2,4,6,8,10}   B = {x : x is a positive even integer and x < 10}
(iv) A = {x : x is a multiple of 10}   B = {10,15,20,25,30,…}▼
(i) A={a,b,c,d} and B={d,c,a,b}
Same four elements: a,b,c,d. Order does not matter in sets.
✅A = B
(ii) A={4,8,12,16} and B={8,4,16,18}
A has 12 — is 12 in B? NO. B has 18 — is 18 in A? NO. Different elements.
❌A ≠ B. A has 12 (not in B); B has 18 (not in A).
(iii) A={2,4,6,8,10} and B={x: positive even integer, x<10}
Convert B: positive even integers less than 10 = {2,4,6,8} A = {2,4,6,8,10} has element 10 which is NOT in B.
❌A ≠ B. A contains 10 but B does not (since 10 is not less than 10).
(iv) A={multiples of 10} and B={10,15,20,25,30,...}
A = {10,20,30,40,...} (only multiples of 10) B = {10,15,20,25,30,...} includes 15, 25, 35... 15 is NOT a multiple of 10, so 15 ∈ B but 15 ∉ A.
❌A ≠ B. B contains 15, 25, 35... which are NOT multiples of 10.
🎯 Tip
  • To prove ≠: find ONE element in one set that is missing from the other
  • To prove =: show both A⊆B and B⊆A (every element matches)
Q4State the reasons for the following:
(i) {1,2,3,…,10} ≠ {x : x ∈ N and 1 < x < 10}
(ii) {2,4,6,8,10} ≠ {x : x = 2n+1 and x ∈ N}
(iii) {5,15,30,45} ≠ {x : x is a multiple of 15}
(iv) {2,3,5,7,9} ≠ {x : x is a prime number}▼
(i) {1,2,...,10} ≠ {x ∈ N, 1 < x < 10}
RHS: 1 < x < 10 means STRICTLY between 1 and 10. RHS = {2,3,4,5,6,7,8,9} — excludes 1 and 10. LHS includes 1 and 10. Different sets.
✅Reason: LHS contains 1 and 10, but RHS uses strict inequalities (1<x<10) which exclude both 1 and 10.
(ii) {2,4,6,8,10} ≠ {x = 2n+1, x ∈ N}
RHS: 2n+1 gives 3,5,7,9,11,... (all ODD numbers). LHS = {2,4,6,8,10} (all EVEN numbers). Completely different.
✅Reason: 2n+1 always produces odd numbers. LHS has only even numbers. No element is common.
(iii) {5,15,30,45} ≠ {multiples of 15}
RHS = {15,30,45,60,75,...} (infinite) LHS has 5 — but 5 is NOT a multiple of 15. LHS is finite, RHS is infinite.
✅Reason: 5 is in LHS but is not a multiple of 15. Also multiples of 15 are infinite, LHS is finite.
(iv) {2,3,5,7,9} ≠ {x : prime numbers}
9 = 3 x 3 → 9 is NOT prime (it is composite). But 9 is in LHS. Also prime numbers are infinite; LHS is finite.
✅Reason: 9 is in LHS but 9 = 3×3 is NOT prime. Also prime numbers form an infinite set.
🎯 Remember
  • 9 is NOT prime (9 = 3×3) — very common mistake!
  • 1 < x < 10 excludes both endpoints (1 and 10)
  • 1 ≤ x ≤ 10 includes both endpoints
Q5List all the subsets of the following sets.
(i) B = {p, q}    (ii) C = {x, y, z}    (iii) D = {a, b, c, d}    (iv) E = {1, 4, 9, 16}    (v) F = {10, 100, 1000}▼
Formula: n elements → 2ⁿ subsets. List: φ, 1-element, 2-element,..., full set.
(i) B = {p, q} — n=2, so 2²=4 subsets
0-element: φ 1-element: {p}, {q} 2-element: {p,q} (the set itself)
✅Subsets: φ, {p}, {q}, {p,q}   (4 subsets)
(ii) C = {x, y, z} — n=3, so 2³=8 subsets
0-element: φ 1-element: {x}, {y}, {z} 2-element: {x,y}, {y,z}, {x,z} 3-element: {x,y,z}
✅φ, {x},{y},{z}, {x,y},{y,z},{x,z}, {x,y,z}   (8 subsets)
(iii) D = {a, b, c, d} — n=4, so 2⁴=16 subsets
0-element (1): φ 1-element (4): {a},{b},{c},{d} 2-element (6): {a,b},{a,c},{a,d},{b,c},{b,d},{c,d} 3-element (4): {a,b,c},{a,b,d},{a,c,d},{b,c,d} 4-element (1): {a,b,c,d} Total: 1+4+6+4+1 = 16 ✓
✅16 subsets total (listed above)
(iv) E = {1, 4, 9, 16} — n=4, so 16 subsets
Same structure as (iii) with elements 1,4,9,16: φ; {1},{4},{9},{16}; {1,4},{1,9},{1,16},{4,9},{4,16},{9,16}; {1,4,9},{1,4,16},{1,9,16},{4,9,16}; {1,4,9,16}
✅16 subsets total
(v) F = {10, 100, 1000} — n=3, so 8 subsets
0-element: φ 1-element: {10},{100},{1000} 2-element: {10,100},{10,1000},{100,1000} 3-element: {10,100,1000}
✅φ, {10},{100},{1000}, {10,100},{10,1000},{100,1000}, {10,100,1000}   (8 subsets)
🎯 Exam Tips for Subsets
  • Number of subsets = 2ⁿ where n = number of elements. Always verify your count!
  • φ is ALWAYS a subset — never forget it!
  • The set itself is also ALWAYS a subset (improper subset)
  • Proper subsets = all subsets except the set itself = 2ⁿ − 1
  • List systematically: 0-element → 1-element → 2-element... to avoid missing any
Telangana State Board · Chapter 2 · Sets

Exercise 2.4 — Finite, Infinite & Empty Sets

Empty sets · Finite sets · Infinite sets · Cardinality n(A)

2 Questions
Key Definitions
  • Empty set (φ): No elements. n(φ) = 0. φ is a FINITE set!
  • Finite set: Elements can be counted; total is a definite whole number
  • Infinite set: Elements go on forever — cannot be completely counted
  • Cardinality n(A): The number of elements in finite set A
  • φ ≠ {0}: φ has NO elements; {0} has ONE element (zero)
Q1State which of the following sets are empty and which are not?
(i) The set of lines passing through a given point.
(ii) Set of odd natural numbers divisible by 2.
(iii) {x : x is a natural number, x < 5 and x > 7}
(iv) {x : x is a common point to any two parallel lines}
(v) Set of even prime numbers.▼

Method: Can you find even ONE element satisfying the condition? YES → NOT empty. Condition is impossible → EMPTY SET.

(i) Set of lines passing through a given point
Try to find one line through a given point. YES — in fact infinitely many lines pass through any point. (Think of a wheel hub — spokes go in all directions.)
❌NOT empty. Infinitely many lines pass through any given point.
(ii) Set of odd natural numbers divisible by 2
Odd number: leaves remainder 1 when divided by 2. Divisible by 2: leaves remainder 0 when divided by 2. A number CANNOT be both odd and divisible by 2. These are contradictory conditions — no such number exists.
✅EMPTY SET (φ). No number can be simultaneously odd and divisible by 2.
(iii) {x : x is a natural number, x < 5 and x > 7}
We need x < 5 (x can be 1,2,3,4) AND x > 7 (x can be 8,9,10,...) No number satisfies BOTH conditions at the same time. The two conditions are mutually contradictory.
✅EMPTY SET (φ). No natural number can be both less than 5 and greater than 7.
(iv) {x : x is a common point to any two parallel lines}
By definition, parallel lines never intersect. They have no point in common. So no such common point exists.
✅EMPTY SET (φ). Parallel lines never meet, so they have no common point.
(v) Set of even prime numbers
Even numbers: 2, 4, 6, 8, ... Prime means: factors are only 1 and itself. 2: factors are 1 and 2 only → prime AND even. 4 = 2×2: has factor 2 in addition to 1 and 4 → not prime. Similarly 6, 8, 10, ... are all not prime. So only 2 is both even and prime.
❌NOT empty. The set = {2}. 2 is the only even prime number.
🎯 Exam Tips
  • Contradictory conditions (odd AND even; x<5 AND x>7) → always empty
  • Geometric impossibilities (parallel lines meeting) → always empty
  • Always justify your answer — don’t just say “empty”
  • {2} is NOT empty — it has one element!
Q2State whether the following sets are finite or infinite.
(i) The set of months in a year.
(ii) {1, 2, 3, …, 99, 100}
(iii) The set of prime numbers smaller than 99.
(iv) The set of letters in the English alphabet.
(v) The set of lines that can be drawn parallel to the X-Axis.
(vi) The set of numbers which are multiples of 5.
(vii) The set of circles passing through the origin (0, 0).▼

FINITE = can count all elements, reaches a definite number. INFINITE = counting never ends, no last element.

(i) Months in a year
Jan, Feb, Mar, Apr, May, Jun, Jul, Aug, Sep, Oct, Nov, Dec. Exactly 12 months. Counting ends.
✅FINITE. n = 12
(ii) {1, 2, 3, …, 99, 100}
Starts at 1, ends at 100. Exactly 100 elements.
✅FINITE. n = 100
(iii) Prime numbers smaller than 99
Primes: 2,3,5,7,11,13,...,97. There are 25 primes below 100. Counting ends at 97.
✅FINITE. n = 25
(iv) Letters in English alphabet
a,b,c,...,z. Exactly 26 letters.
✅FINITE. n = 26
(v) Lines parallel to X-axis
For every real number k, the line y = k is parallel to X-axis. Real numbers are infinite, so infinitely many such lines exist.
✅INFINITE. For every real k, y=k is parallel to X-axis. Infinitely many exist.
(vi) Multiples of 5
5, 10, 15, 20, 25, ... continues forever. For any n, 5n is a multiple. No last element.
✅INFINITE. Multiples of 5 have no last element.
(vii) Circles passing through origin (0,0)
A circle with any centre (a,b) and radius r = √(a²+b²) passes through origin. Infinitely many choices of centre exist.
✅INFINITE. Infinitely many circles can pass through the origin.
🎯 Quick Classification Guide
  • Days, months, letters, seasons, planets → FINITE
  • Multiples, factors (of all numbers), integers, rationals → INFINITE
  • Lines/circles through a point, parallel to a line → INFINITE
  • φ (empty set) is FINITE with 0 elements — asked in exams!

Master Cheat Sheet — Chapter 2: Sets

SYMBOLS & BASICS

  • ∈ = belongs to   ∉ = does not belong to
  • ⊆ = subset of   φ = empty set
  • N ⊂ W ⊂ ℤ ⊂ ℚ ⊂ ℝ
  • SET = well-defined collection
  • Opinion words (best, most) → NOT a set

OPERATIONS

  • A ∪ B = A OR B (everything)
  • A ∩ B = A AND B (common only)
  • A−B = in A, NOT in B
  • A−B ≠ B−A (order matters!)
  • Disjoint: A ∩ B = φ

EQUAL SETS & SUBSETS

  • A=B means A⊆B AND B⊆A
  • φ ⊆ every set (always!)
  • A ⊆ A (every set is its own subset)
  • n elements → 2ⁿ subsets total
  • If A⊆B: A∪B=B and A∩B=A

FINITE / INFINITE / EMPTY

  • Empty φ: NO elements, n(φ)=0, FINITE
  • φ ≠ {0} (they are different!)
  • 2 is ONLY even prime number
  • Even ∩ Odd = φ (always disjoint)
  • 9 = 3×3 is NOT prime!
Telangana State Board · Chapter 2 · Sets

Chapter 2 — Master Cheat Sheet

All key definitions, symbols, rules and exam facts from Chapter 2 — Sets. Revise this before your exam!

Quick Revision All Topics
✎

Symbols & Basic Definitions

The language of sets — know these perfectly

SET SYMBOLS

  • ∈   "belongs to" (element in set)
  • ∉   "does not belong to"
  • ⊆   "is a subset of"
  • ¬⊆   "is not a subset of"
  • φ or {}   empty set (null set)
  • μ or U   universal set (rectangle in Venn)

NUMBER SETS HIERARCHY

  • N = Natural = {1,2,3,...}
  • W = Whole = {0,1,2,3,...}
  • ℤ = Integers = {...,−2,−1,0,1,2,...}
  • ℚ = Rational = all p/q fractions
  • ℝ = Real = all numbers
  • Order: N ⊂ W ⊂ ℤ ⊂ ℚ ⊂ ℝ

FORMS OF A SET

  • Roster form: List all elements in { }
  • e.g. A = {1, 2, 3, 4}
  • Set-builder form: Describe by rule
  • e.g. A = {x : x ∈ N, x ≤ 4}
  • Elements are DISTINCT (no repeats)
  • Order does NOT matter in a set

WHAT MAKES A SET?

  • Must be well-defined
  • Can clearly say: belongs or not
  • Opinion words → NOT a set:
  • best, most, talented, beautiful...
  • Clear rule → IS a set:
  • all even numbers, all months with J...
⊖

Set Operations

Union, Intersection, Difference — with rules

UNION A ∪ B

  • All elements in A OR B (or both)
  • A ∪ B = {x : x∈A or x∈B}
  • No element written twice
  • A ∪ φ = A
  • A ∪ A = A
  • If A⊆B → A ∪ B = B

INTERSECTION A ∩ B

  • Only elements in BOTH A AND B
  • A ∩ B = {x : x∈A and x∈B}
  • A ∩ B = B ∩ A (commutative!)
  • A ∩ φ = φ
  • A ∩ A = A
  • If A⊆B → A ∩ B = A

DIFFERENCE A − B

  • Elements in A but NOT in B
  • A−B = {x : x∈A and x∉B}
  • A−B ≠ B−A (order matters!)
  • Remove A∩B from A to get A−B
  • Remove A∩B from B to get B−A
  • A−B, B−A, A∩B are mutually disjoint

DISJOINT SETS

  • A ∩ B = φ → disjoint
  • No element common to both
  • Even ∩ Odd = φ (always!)
  • 2 is ONLY even prime number
  • Represented as separate circles in Venn
⊆

Subsets & Equal Sets

Rules, formulas and important properties

SUBSET RULES

  • φ ⊆ every set (always!)
  • Every set ⊆ itself: A ⊆ A
  • A ⊆ B and B ⊆ C → A ⊆ C
  • If A⊆B → A∪B=B and A∩B=A
  • Proper subset: A⊂B means A⊆B but A≠B

NUMBER OF SUBSETS

  • n elements → 2ⁿ subsets total
  • n=0 (φ) → 1 subset (just φ)
  • n=1 → 2 subsets
  • n=2 → 4 subsets
  • n=3 → 8 subsets
  • n=4 → 16 subsets

EQUAL SETS

  • A = B means A⊆B AND B⊆A
  • Same elements, any order
  • {1,2,3} = {3,1,2} = {2,3,1}
  • To prove ≠: find 1 element in one not other
  • To prove =: show every element matches both ways

LISTING SUBSETS SYSTEMATICALLY

  • Step 1: Write φ
  • Step 2: All 1-element subsets
  • Step 3: All 2-element subsets
  • Step 4: Continue... up to full set
  • Verify count = 2ⁿ at the end
n(A)

Finite, Infinite & Empty Sets

Classification rules and cardinality

EMPTY SET

  • φ = {} = set with NO elements
  • n(φ) = 0
  • φ is a FINITE set (0 elements)
  • φ ≠ {0}   (different things!)
  • φ ⊆ every set
  • Contradictory conditions → empty

FINITE SETS

  • Counting ends at a definite number
  • Examples: months, alphabet, days
  • Primes less than 100 (= 25 primes)
  • {1,2,3,...,100}: n = 100
  • Any set with specific upper limit

INFINITE SETS

  • Counting never ends
  • Examples: N, W, ℤ, ℚ, ℝ
  • All multiples of any number
  • Lines parallel to X-axis
  • Circles through a given point

QUICK EXAM FACTS

  • 2 = ONLY even prime
  • 9 = 3×3 is NOT prime!
  • 1 is NOT a prime number
  • Even ∩ Odd = φ always
  • n(A∪B) = n(A)+n(B)−n(A∩B)
  • If disjoint: n(A∪B) = n(A)+n(B)
📚
