Telangana State Board · Chapter 1 · Real Numbers

Chapter 1 — Master Cheat Sheet

All key formulas, theorems, rules and exam facts from Chapter 1. Revise before your exam!

Quick RevisionAll 5 Exercises

Master Cheat Sheet — Chapter 1: Real Numbers

EX 1.1 — EUCLID

  • Formula: a = bq + r (0 ≤ r < b)
  • Start with LARGER number as a (dividend)
  • HCF = divisor when remainder first = 0
  • Write every step as a full equation
  • Odd integer proof: b=6 → odd remainders = 1,3,5
  • Square → 3p or 3p+1   (NEVER 3p+2)
  • Cube → 9m, 9m+1, or 9m+8

EX 1.2 — PRIMES

  • HCF = LOWEST powers of COMMON primes
  • LCM = HIGHEST powers of ALL primes
  • HCF × LCM = a × b (TWO numbers ONLY!)
  • 6n = 2n×3n: no factor 5 → never ends in 0
  • 6 raised to any power always ends in 6
  • Composite proof: factor out common → A×B form

EX 1.3 — DECIMALS

  • q = 2n×5m only → Terminating
  • q has other prime → Non-Terminating Repeating
  • SIMPLIFY fraction FIRST, then check q!
  • Th.1.4: multiply to make denominator = 10n
  • Repeating decimal: multiply by 10n, subtract, solve
  • Observation: Terminating → q has only 2,5; NTR → other primes

EX 1.4 & 1.5

  • Irrationals: PROOF BY CONTRADICTION
  • Assume rational → a/b (co-prime) → rearrange → √prime = rational → contradiction ∎
  • ALWAYS name Theorem 1.6 by name!
  • logaN = x ⇔ ax = N
  • Product / Quotient / Power rules
  • log 2 = irrational   log 100 = 2 = rational
Telangana State Board · Chapter 3 · Polynomials

Exercise 3.1 — Polynomials — Basics

Degree · Coefficients · Constant term · Values of polynomial · Zeroes

Degree & Coefficients 5 Questions
Key Definitions from Book
  • Polynomial in x: Expression of the form axn + bxn−1 + ... where n is a whole number and a ≠ 0
  • Degree: The highest power of x in the polynomial
  • Coefficient: The number multiplying each power of x
  • Constant term: The term with no variable (power of x = 0)
  • Value p(k): Replace x by k and calculate — if p(k) = 0 then k is a ZERO of the polynomial
  • Linear: degree 1   Quadratic: degree 2   Cubic: degree 3
Q1In p(x) = 5x7 − 6x5 + 7x − 6, what is the
(i) coefficient of x5    (ii) degree of p(x)    (iii) constant term.▼

Method: Read off the required parts directly from the polynomial p(x) = 5x7 − 6x5 + 7x − 6.

(i) Coefficient of x5
p(x) = 5x&sup7; − 6x&sup5; + 7x − 6 The term with x&sup5; is: −6x&sup5; The number multiplying x&sup5; is −6.
✅Coefficient of x5 = −6
(ii) Degree of p(x)
p(x) = 5x&sup7; − 6x&sup5; + 7x − 6 The powers of x are: 7, 5, 1, 0. The highest power is 7.
✅Degree of p(x) = 7
(iii) Constant term
p(x) = 5x&sup7; − 6x&sup5; + 7x − 6 The constant term is the term with no x (x&sup0; = 1). Here it is −6 (i.e., −6 × x&sup0; = −6).
✅Constant term = −6
🎯 Exam Tips
  • Coefficient includes the sign (+ or −) — coefficient of x&sup5; is −6, not 6
  • Degree = highest power of the variable in the polynomial
  • Constant term = the number with no variable (the last standalone number)
Q2State which of the following statements are true and which are false? Give reasons.
(i) The degree of the polynomial √2x2 − 3x + 1 is √2.
(ii) The coefficient of x2 in p(x) = 3x3 − 4x2 + 5x + 7 is 2.
(iii) The degree of a constant term is zero.
(iv) 1/(x2 − 5x + 6) is a quadratic polynomial.
(v) The degree of a polynomial is one more than the number of terms in it.▼

Method: Apply definitions carefully. For each statement, identify the specific error or confirm it is correct.

(i) The degree of √2x² − 3x + 1 is √2
The degree is the HIGHEST POWER of x, not the coefficient. In √2x² − 3x + 1, the highest power of x is 2. The √2 is the COEFFICIENT of x², not the degree.
❌FALSE. The degree is 2 (the highest power of x). √2 is the coefficient of x², not the degree.
(ii) Coefficient of x² in 3x³ − 4x² + 5x + 7 is 2
In p(x) = 3x³ − 4x² + 5x + 7 The term with x² is: −4x² So the coefficient of x² is −4, not 2.
❌FALSE. The coefficient of x² is −4 (not 2). The sign must be included.
(iii) The degree of a constant term is zero
A constant like 6 can be written as 6 × x&sup0; = 6. The power of x is 0. So the degree of any constant term is 0.
✅TRUE. Any constant can be written as c × x&sup0;, so its degree is 0.
(iv) 1/(x² − 5x + 6) is a quadratic polynomial
A polynomial in x has the form ax⊃n + bx⊃n−1 + ... where n is a WHOLE NUMBER. In 1/(x² − 5x + 6), the variable x is in the DENOMINATOR. This means x appears with a NEGATIVE power (x⊃−²), which is not a whole number. So it is NOT a polynomial at all.
❌FALSE. This is not a polynomial because x is in the denominator (negative powers of x are not allowed in polynomials).
(v) Degree of a polynomial is one more than the number of terms
Counter-example: p(x) = x² + 1 Number of terms = 2. Degree = 2. But 2 ≠ 2+1 = 3. Another: p(x) = x³ + x² + x + 1 Number of terms = 4. Degree = 3. And 3 ≠ 4+1 = 5. There is NO such rule between degree and number of terms.
❌FALSE. Degree and number of terms are independent. Example: x² + 1 has degree 2 and 2 terms; but degree ≠ terms + 1.
🎯 Exam Tips — Common Mistakes to Avoid
  • Degree = highest POWER, not coefficient — most common error!
  • Coefficient includes the negative sign: in −4x², coefficient is −4
  • Expressions with x in denominator are NOT polynomials
  • Expressions with fractional or negative powers of x are NOT polynomials
Q3If p(t) = t3 − 1, find the values of p(1), p(−1), p(0), p(2), p(−2).▼

Method: Replace t with the given value, then calculate. p(k) means substitute t = k into the expression.

p(t) = t³ − 1 p(1) = (1)³ − 1 = 1 − 1 = 0 p(−1) = (−1)³ − 1 = −1 − 1 = −2 p(0) = (0)³ − 1 = 0 − 1 = −1 p(2) = (2)³ − 1 = 8 − 1 = 7 p(−2) = (−2)³ − 1 = −8 − 1 = −9
✅p(1) = 0, p(−1) = −2, p(0) = −1, p(2) = 7, p(−2) = −9

Notice: p(1) = 0. This means t = 1 is a zero of p(t) = t³ − 1. A value k where p(k) = 0 is called a zero of the polynomial.

🎯 Exam Tip
  • (−1)³ = −1 (odd power keeps the negative sign)
  • (−2)³ = −8 (not +8)
  • If p(k) = 0, always state “k is a zero of the polynomial”
Q4Check whether −2 and 2 are the zeroes of the polynomial x4 − 16.▼

Method: Substitute each value into p(x). If p(k) = 0, then k IS a zero. If p(k) ≠ 0, then k is NOT a zero.

Let p(x) = x&sup4; − 16 Check x = −2: p(−2) = (−2)&sup4; − 16 = 16 − 16 = 0 [since (−2)&sup4; = 16] Since p(−2) = 0, −2 IS a zero ✓ Check x = 2: p(2) = (2)&sup4; − 16 = 16 − 16 = 0 Since p(2) = 0, 2 IS a zero ✓
✅Yes, both −2 and 2 are zeroes of the polynomial x&sup4; − 16.

Why? Because x&sup4; − 16 = (x² − 4)(x² + 4) = (x−2)(x+2)(x²+4). So ±2 are indeed zeroes.

🎯 Note on Even Powers
  • (−2)&sup4; = +16 (even power always gives positive)
  • (−2)³ = −8 (odd power keeps negative sign)
  • Always show your substitution clearly — don’t just write the answer
Q5Check whether 3 and −2 are the zeroes of the polynomial p(x) when p(x) = x2 − x − 6.▼

Method: Find p(3) and p(−2). If both equal 0, both are zeroes.

p(x) = x² − x − 6 Check x = 3: p(3) = (3)² − 3 − 6 = 9 − 3 − 6 = 0 ✓ Since p(3) = 0, 3 IS a zero Check x = −2: p(−2) = (−2)² − (−2) − 6 = 4 + 2 − 6 = 0 ✓ Since p(−2) = 0, −2 IS a zero
✅Yes, both 3 and −2 are zeroes of p(x) = x² − x − 6.

Verify: x² − x − 6 = (x − 3)(x + 2). So zeroes are x = 3 and x = −2. ✓

🎯 Exam Tip

When checking, always write the full substitution: p(−2) = (−2)² − (−2) − 6. Be careful with −(−2) = +2. Sign errors are the most common mistake here!

Telangana State Board · Chapter 3 · Polynomials

Exercise 3.2 — Zeroes from Graphs & Algebraically

Geometrical meaning of zeroes · Number of zeroes from graph · Finding zeroes algebraically

Graph & Zeroes 4 Questions
Key Geometric Rule from Book
  • The zeroes of p(x) are the x-coordinates of the points where the graph of y = p(x) intersects the X-axis
  • A polynomial of degree n has at most n zeroes
  • Linear (degree 1): at most 1 zero   Quadratic (degree 2): at most 2 zeroes   Cubic (degree 3): at most 3 zeroes
  • The number of zeroes = number of times the graph crosses or touches the X-axis
Q1The graphs of y = p(x) are given in the figures below, for some polynomials p(x). In each case, find the number of zeroes of p(x).
(i) through (vi) — six different graphs▼

Method: Count how many times the graph CROSSES or TOUCHES the X-axis. Each such point gives one zero of p(x). Touching without crossing = one zero (repeated).

📚 Exact graphs from your T.S. Govt. Textbook (Page 64)
How to Count Zeroes from Each Graph

Count how many times the curve crosses or touches the X-axis. Each such point = one zero.

✅(i) 0 zeroes   (ii) 1 zero   (iii) 3 zeroes   (iv) 2 zeroes   (v) 4 zeroes   (vi) 3 zeroes
🎯 Key Rule — Reading Zeroes from a Graph
  • Each point where curve crosses the X-axis = 1 distinct zero
  • If curve touches X-axis without crossing (like graph ii) = 1 repeated zero
  • If curve is entirely above or below X-axis (like graph i) = 0 real zeroes
  • A polynomial of degree n can have AT MOST n zeroes
Q2Find the zeroes of the given polynomials.
(i) p(x) = 3x    (ii) p(x) = x2 + 5x + 6    (iii) p(x) = (x+2)(x+3)    (iv) p(x) = x4 − 16▼

Method: Set p(x) = 0. Solve for x. Each solution is a zero.

(i) p(x) = 3x
Set p(x) = 0: 3x = 0 x = 0
✅Zero of p(x) = 3x is x = 0
(ii) p(x) = x² + 5x + 6
Set p(x) = 0: x² + 5x + 6 = 0 Factorise by splitting middle term: 5x = 2x + 3x, product = 6 (x + 2)(x + 3) = 0 x = −2 or x = −3
✅Zeroes: x = −2 and x = −3
(iii) p(x) = (x+2)(x+3)
This is already in factored form. Set p(x) = 0: (x+2)(x+3) = 0 x+2 = 0 → x = −2 x+3 = 0 → x = −3 [Same answer as (ii) because (x+2)(x+3) = x²+5x+6]
✅Zeroes: x = −2 and x = −3
(iv) p(x) = x&sup4; − 16
Set p(x) = 0: x&sup4; − 16 = 0 x&sup4; = 16 x² = ±4 Case 1: x² = 4 → x = ±2 (real zeroes) Case 2: x² = −4 → no real solution (square of real number can't be negative) Real zeroes: x = 2 and x = −2
✅Zeroes: x = 2 and x = −2   (two real zeroes)
🎯 Factoring Techniques
  • For quadratics: use splitting the middle term or the quadratic formula
  • a² − b² = (a−b)(a+b) — use this identity for difference of squares
  • x&sup4; − 16 = (x²)² − 4² = (x²−4)(x²+4) = (x−2)(x+2)(x²+4)
Q3Draw the graphs of the given polynomial and find the zeroes. Justify the answers.
(i) p(x) = x2 − x − 12    (ii) p(x) = x2 − 6x + 9    (iii) p(x) = x2 − 4x + 5    (iv) p(x) = x2 + 3x − 4    (v) p(x) = x2 − 1▼

Method: Find zeroes algebraically by factoring. The graph intersects X-axis at those x-values. Verify the connection between graph and zeroes.

(i) p(x) = x² − x − 12
Set p(x) = 0: x² − x − 12 = 0 Split middle term: −x = −4x + 3x, product = −12 (x − 4)(x + 3) = 0 x = 4 or x = −3 Graph: parabola opening UPWARD (a = 1 > 0) Crosses X-axis at x = −3 and x = 4
✅Zeroes: x = 4 and x = −3. Graph crosses X-axis at (−3, 0) and (4, 0).
(ii) p(x) = x² − 6x + 9
x² − 6x + 9 = (x − 3)² = 0 x = 3 (repeated zero) Graph: parabola opening UPWARD, TOUCHES X-axis at x = 3 (does not cross)
✅Zero: x = 3 (repeated). Graph touches X-axis at (3, 0) without crossing.
(iii) p(x) = x² − 4x + 5
Discriminant = b² − 4ac = (−4)² − 4(1)(5) = 16 − 20 = −4 < 0 Since discriminant < 0, there are NO real zeroes. Graph: parabola opening UPWARD, does NOT intersect X-axis (entirely above X-axis)
✅No real zeroes. Graph is entirely above X-axis.
(iv) p(x) = x² + 3x − 4
x² + 3x − 4 = 0 Split: 3x = 4x − x, product = −4 (x + 4)(x − 1) = 0 x = −4 or x = 1 Graph: parabola opening UPWARD, crosses X-axis at x = −4 and x = 1
✅Zeroes: x = −4 and x = 1. Graph crosses X-axis at (−4, 0) and (1, 0).
(v) p(x) = x² − 1
x² − 1 = 0 (x − 1)(x + 1) = 0 [difference of squares: a² − b² = (a−b)(a+b)] x = 1 or x = −1 Graph: parabola opening UPWARD, crosses X-axis at x = −1 and x = 1
✅Zeroes: x = 1 and x = −1. Graph crosses X-axis at (−1, 0) and (1, 0).
🎯 Three Cases for Quadratic Graph — From Your Book
  • 2 distinct zeroes: Parabola crosses X-axis at 2 points (discriminant > 0)
  • 1 repeated zero: Parabola TOUCHES X-axis at 1 point, does not cross (discriminant = 0)
  • 0 real zeroes: Parabola entirely above or below X-axis (discriminant < 0)
Q4Why are 1/4 and −1 zeroes of the polynomial p(x) = 4x2 + 3x − 1?▼

Method: A value k is a zero of p(x) if and only if p(k) = 0. Verify by substituting each value.

p(x) = 4x² + 3x − 1 Check x = 1/4: p(1/4) = 4(1/4)² + 3(1/4) − 1 = 4(1/16) + 3/4 − 1 = 1/4 + 3/4 − 1 = 4/4 − 1 = 1 − 1 = 0 ✓ Check x = −1: p(−1) = 4(−1)² + 3(−1) − 1 = 4(1) − 3 − 1 = 4 − 3 − 1 = 0 ✓
✅Since p(1/4) = 0 and p(−1) = 0, both 1/4 and −1 are zeroes of p(x) = 4x² + 3x − 1.

A value k is a zero of p(x) if and only if p(k) = 0. This is the definition of a zero of a polynomial.

🎯 Exam Tip

This is a “verify” question. Show the full substitution and arithmetic for EACH value. Write the final statement: “Since p(k) = 0, k is a zero of p(x).”

Telangana State Board · Chapter 3 · Polynomials

Exercise 3.3 — Zeroes and Coefficients

Sum & Product of zeroes · Forming quadratic polynomials · Cubic polynomial relations

Sum & Product 4 Questions
Key Formulas from Book — Relationships Between Zeroes and Coefficients

For quadratic polynomial ax² + bx + c with zeroes α and β:

  • Sum of zeroes: α + β = −b/a = −(coefficient of x) / (coefficient of x²)
  • Product of zeroes: αβ = c/a = (constant term) / (coefficient of x²)
  • Forming quadratic: x² − (α+β)x + αβ = 0

For cubic polynomial ax³ + bx² + cx + d with zeroes α, β, γ:

  • α+β+γ = −b/a    αβ+βγ+γα = c/a    αβγ = −d/a
Q1Find the zeroes of the following quadratic polynomials and verify the relationship between the zeroes and the coefficients.
(i) x2 − 2x − 8   (ii) 4s2 − 4s + 1   (iii) 6x2 − 3 − 7x   (iv) 4u2 + 8u   (v) t2 − 15   (vi) 3x2 − x − 4▼

Method: (1) Factorise to find zeroes. (2) Calculate sum and product of zeroes. (3) Verify using −b/a and c/a.

(i) x² − 2x − 8   [a=1, b=−2, c=−8]
x² − 2x − 8 = (x − 4)(x + 2) = 0 Zeroes: α = 4, β = −2 Sum: α+β = 4+(−2) = 2   Check: −b/a = −(−2)/1 = 2 ✓ Product: αβ = 4×(−2) = −8   Check: c/a = −8/1 = −8 ✓
✅Zeroes: 4 and −2. Sum = 2 = −b/a ✓   Product = −8 = c/a ✓
(ii) 4s² − 4s + 1   [a=4, b=−4, c=1]
4s² − 4s + 1 = (2s − 1)² = 0 s = 1/2 (repeated zero) α = β = 1/2 Sum: 1/2 + 1/2 = 1   Check: −b/a = −(−4)/4 = 1 ✓ Product: (1/2)(1/2) = 1/4   Check: c/a = 1/4 ✓
✅Zero: 1/2 (repeated). Sum = 1 = −b/a ✓   Product = 1/4 = c/a ✓
(iii) 6x² − 3 − 7x = 6x² − 7x − 3   [a=6, b=−7, c=−3]
6x² − 7x − 3 = 0 Split −7x: −9x + 2x (product = −18, matches 6×−3) = (6x² − 9x) + (2x − 3) = 3x(2x − 3) + 1(2x − 3) = (3x + 1)(2x − 3) = 0 α = −1/3, β = 3/2 Sum: −1/3 + 3/2 = −2/6 + 9/6 = 7/6   Check: −b/a = −(−7)/6 = 7/6 ✓ Product: (−1/3)(3/2) = −1/2   Check: c/a = −3/6 = −1/2 ✓
✅Zeroes: −1/3 and 3/2. Verified ✓
(iv) 4u² + 8u   [a=4, b=8, c=0]
4u² + 8u = 4u(u + 2) = 0 α = 0, β = −2 Sum: 0+(−2) = −2   Check: −b/a = −8/4 = −2 ✓ Product: 0×(−2) = 0   Check: c/a = 0/4 = 0 ✓
✅Zeroes: 0 and −2. Verified ✓
(v) t² − 15   [a=1, b=0, c=−15]
t² − 15 = 0 t² = 15 t = √15 or t = −√15 Sum: √15 + (−√15) = 0   Check: −b/a = 0/1 = 0 ✓ Product: √15 × (−√15) = −15   Check: c/a = −15/1 = −15 ✓
✅Zeroes: √15 and −√15. Verified ✓
(vi) 3x² − x − 4   [a=3, b=−1, c=−4]
3x² − x − 4 = 0 Split −x: −4x + 3x (product = −12, matches 3×−4) = (3x² − 4x) + (3x − 4) = x(3x − 4) + 1(3x − 4) = (x + 1)(3x − 4) = 0 α = −1, β = 4/3 Sum: −1 + 4/3 = −3/3 + 4/3 = 1/3   Check: −b/a = −(−1)/3 = 1/3 ✓ Product: (−1)(4/3) = −4/3   Check: c/a = −4/3 ✓
✅Zeroes: −1 and 4/3. Verified ✓
🎯 Verification Format for Full Marks
  • Always write: Sum = α+β = value   and   −b/a = value   then ✓
  • Always write: Product = αβ = value   and   c/a = value   then ✓
  • This verification format gets full marks in the board exam
Q2Find the quadratic polynomial in each case, with the given numbers as the sum and product of its zeroes respectively.
(i) 1/4, −1   (ii) √2, 1/3   (iii) 0, √5   (iv) 1, 1   (v) −1/4, 1/4   (vi) 4, 1▼

Method: If sum = S and product = P, then quadratic polynomial = k[x² − Sx + P]. For simplest polynomial, take k = 1 (or clear fractions).

(i) Sum = 1/4, Product = −1
p(x) = x² − (Sum)x + Product = x² − (1/4)x + (−1) = x² − x/4 − 1 Multiply by 4 to clear fraction: = 4x² − x − 4
✅Quadratic polynomial: 4x² − x − 4
(ii) Sum = √2, Product = 1/3
p(x) = x² − √2 x + 1/3 Multiply by 3: 3x² − 3√2 x + 1
✅Quadratic polynomial: 3x² − 3√2 x + 1
(iii) Sum = 0, Product = √5
p(x) = x² − (0)x + √5 = x² + √5
✅Quadratic polynomial: x² + √5
(iv) Sum = 1, Product = 1
p(x) = x² − 1⋅x + 1 = x² − x + 1
✅Quadratic polynomial: x² − x + 1
(v) Sum = −1/4, Product = 1/4
p(x) = x² − (−1/4)x + 1/4 = x² + x/4 + 1/4 Multiply by 4: 4x² + x + 1
✅Quadratic polynomial: 4x² + x + 1
(vi) Sum = 4, Product = 1
p(x) = x² − 4x + 1
✅Quadratic polynomial: x² − 4x + 1
🎯 Formula to Memorise
  • Quadratic with zeroes α and β: x² − (α+β)x + αβ
  • If answer has fractions, multiply throughout to clear them
  • Any constant multiple of this polynomial is also valid: kx² − k(α+β)x + kαβ
Q3Find the quadratic polynomial, for the zeroes α, β given in each case.
(i) 2, −1    (ii) √3, −√3    (iii) 1/4, −1    (iv) 1/2, 3/2▼

Method: Calculate Sum = α+β and Product = αβ. Then form p(x) = x² − (Sum)x + Product.

(i) Zeroes: 2, −1
Sum = 2 + (−1) = 1 Product = 2 × (−1) = −2 p(x) = x² − (1)x + (−2) = x² − x − 2
✅x² − x − 2
(ii) Zeroes: √3, −√3
Sum = √3 + (−√3) = 0 Product = √3 × (−√3) = −3 p(x) = x² − 0 + (−3) = x² − 3
✅x² − 3
(iii) Zeroes: 1/4, −1
Sum = 1/4 + (−1) = 1/4 − 4/4 = −3/4 Product = (1/4)(−1) = −1/4 p(x) = x² − (−3/4)x + (−1/4) = x² + 3x/4 − 1/4 Multiply by 4: 4x² + 3x − 1
✅4x² + 3x − 1
(iv) Zeroes: 1/2, 3/2
Sum = 1/2 + 3/2 = 4/2 = 2 Product = (1/2)(3/2) = 3/4 p(x) = x² − 2x + 3/4 Multiply by 4: 4x² − 8x + 3
✅4x² − 8x + 3
🎯 Exam Tip
  • Always multiply by LCM of denominators at the end to give integer coefficients
  • Verify by checking: do the zeroes of your answer match the given zeroes?
Q4Verify that 1, −1 and +3 are the zeroes of the cubic polynomial x3 − 3x2 − x + 3 and check the relationship between zeroes and the coefficients.▼

Method: (1) Verify by substitution. (2) Use α+β+γ = −b/a, αβ+βγ+γα = c/a, αβγ = −d/a.

p(x) = x³ − 3x² − x + 3 [a=1, b=−3, c=−1, d=3] Step 1: Verify zeroes p(1) = 1 − 3 − 1 + 3 = 0 ✓ p(−1) = (−1)³ − 3(−1)² − (−1) + 3 = −1 − 3 + 1 + 3 = 0 ✓ p(3) = 27 − 27 − 3 + 3 = 0 ✓ So α=1, β=−1, γ=3 are zeroes. ✓ Step 2: Verify relationships α+β+γ = 1+(−1)+3 = 3 Check: −b/a = −(−3)/1 = 3 ✓ αβ+βγ+γα = (1)(−1)+(−1)(3)+(3)(1) = −1 − 3 + 3 = −1 Check: c/a = −1/1 = −1 ✓ αβγ = (1)(−1)(3) = −3 Check: −d/a = −3/1 = −3 ✓
✅1, −1 and 3 are verified zeroes. All three coefficient relationships hold. ∎
🎯 Cubic Formulas — Write These in Exam
  • α+β+γ = −b/a
  • αβ+βγ+γα = c/a
  • αβγ = −d/a
  • Always verify all three — examiners check for all three relationships!
Telangana State Board · Chapter 3 · Polynomials

Exercise 3.4 — Division Algorithm for Polynomials

Polynomial long division · p(x) = g(x)×q(x) + r(x) · Finding unknown polynomials

Division Algorithm 5 Questions
Division Algorithm for Polynomials (from Book)

If p(x) and g(x) are polynomials with g(x) ≠ 0, then:

p(x) = g(x) × q(x) + r(x)

where r(x) = 0 OR degree of r(x) < degree of g(x)

  • p(x) = Dividend   g(x) = Divisor   q(x) = Quotient   r(x) = Remainder
  • Always write polynomials in STANDARD FORM (decreasing powers) before dividing
  • Divide highest degree term of dividend by highest degree term of divisor at each step
Q1Divide the polynomial p(x) by the polynomial g(x) and find the quotient and remainder in each of the following:
(i) p(x) = x3 − 3x2 + 5x − 3, g(x) = x2 − 2
(ii) p(x) = x4 − 3x2 + 4x + 5, g(x) = x2 + 1 − x
(iii) p(x) = x4 − 5x + 6, g(x) = 2 − x2▼

Method: (1) Write both polynomials in standard form (decreasing powers). (2) Divide highest-degree term of dividend by highest-degree term of divisor to get first term of quotient. (3) Multiply and subtract. (4) Repeat until degree of remainder < degree of divisor.

(i) p(x) = x³ − 3x² + 5x − 3 ÷ g(x) = x² − 2
Divide: x³ ÷ x² = x → first term of quotient is x Step 1: x × (x² − 2) = x³ − 2x Subtract: (x³ − 3x² + 5x − 3) − (x³ − 2x) = −3x² + 7x − 3 Step 2: −3x² ÷ x² = −3 → next term of quotient is −3 −3 × (x² − 2) = −3x² + 6 Subtract: (−3x² + 7x − 3) − (−3x² + 6) = 7x − 9 Degree of (7x − 9) = 1 < degree of g(x) = 2. STOP. Quotient q(x) = x − 3 Remainder r(x) = 7x − 9 Verify: (x²−2)(x−3) + (7x−9) = x³−3x²−2x+6+7x−9 = x³−3x²+5x−3 ✓
✅Quotient = x − 3    Remainder = 7x − 9
(ii) p(x) = x&sup4; − 3x² + 4x + 5 ÷ g(x) = x² − x + 1 (standard form)
Write g(x) in standard form: x² + 1 − x = x² − x + 1 Step 1: x&sup4; ÷ x² = x² x²(x²−x+1) = x&sup4; − x³ + x² Remainder: (x&sup4;−3x²+4x+5) − (x&sup4;−x³+x²) = x³ − 4x² + 4x + 5 Step 2: x³ ÷ x² = x x(x²−x+1) = x³ − x² + x Remainder: (x³−4x²+4x+5) − (x³−x²+x) = −3x² + 3x + 5 Step 3: −3x² ÷ x² = −3 −3(x²−x+1) = −3x² + 3x − 3 Remainder: (−3x²+3x+5) − (−3x²+3x−3) = 8 Quotient = x² + x − 3   Remainder = 8
✅Quotient = x² + x − 3    Remainder = 8
(iii) p(x) = x&sup4; − 5x + 6 ÷ g(x) = −x² + 2 (standard form)
Write g(x) in standard form: 2 − x² = −x² + 2 Write p(x) fully: x&sup4; + 0x³ + 0x² − 5x + 6 Step 1: x&sup4; ÷ (−x²) = −x² −x²(−x²+2) = x&sup4; − 2x² Remainder: (x&sup4;−5x+6) − (x&sup4;−2x²) = 2x² − 5x + 6 Step 2: 2x² ÷ (−x²) = −2 −2(−x²+2) = 2x² − 4 Remainder: (2x²−5x+6) − (2x²−4) = −5x + 10 Quotient = −x² − 2   Remainder = −5x + 10
✅Quotient = −x² − 2    Remainder = −5x + 10
🎯 Division Algorithm Tips
  • ALWAYS write both polynomials in STANDARD FORM (decreasing powers) first
  • Insert 0 coefficients for missing powers: x&sup4;+0x³+0x²−5x+6
  • Stop when degree of remainder < degree of divisor
  • Always verify: p(x) = g(x)×q(x) + r(x). This earns marks!
Q2Check in which case the first polynomial is a factor of the second polynomial by dividing the second polynomial by the first polynomial:
(i) t2 − 3,   2t4 + 3t3 − 2t2 − 9t − 12
(ii) x2 + 3x + 1,   3x4 + 5x3 − 7x2 + 2x + 2
(iii) x3 − 3x + 1,   x5 − 4x3 + x2 + 3x + 1▼

Method: Divide the second polynomial by the first. If remainder = 0, then the first IS a factor. If remainder ≠ 0, it is NOT a factor.

(i) Divide 2t&sup4; + 3t³ − 2t² − 9t − 12 by t² − 3
Dividing step by step: 2t&sup4; ÷ t² = 2t² 2t²(t²−3) = 2t&sup4;−6t² Remainder: 3t³+4t²−9t−12 3t³ ÷ t² = 3t 3t(t²−3) = 3t³−9t Remainder: 4t²−12 4t² ÷ t² = 4 4(t²−3) = 4t²−12 Remainder: 0 Remainder = 0, so t²−3 IS a factor.
✅YES — t² − 3 is a factor of 2t&sup4; + 3t³ − 2t² − 9t − 12
(ii) Divide 3x&sup4; + 5x³ − 7x² + 2x + 2 by x² + 3x + 1
Quotient: 3x² − 4x + 2 Remainder: 0 Remainder = 0, so x²+3x+1 IS a factor.
✅YES — x² + 3x + 1 is a factor of 3x&sup4; + 5x³ − 7x² + 2x + 2
(iii) Divide x&sup5; − 4x³ + x² + 3x + 1 by x³ − 3x + 1
Quotient: x² − 1 Remainder: 2 Remainder = 2 ≠ 0, so x³−3x+1 is NOT a factor.
✅NO — x³ − 3x + 1 is NOT a factor (remainder = 2)
🎯 Factor Test
  • g(x) is a factor of p(x) if and only if remainder = 0 when p(x) is divided by g(x)
  • This is equivalent to: all zeroes of g(x) are also zeroes of p(x)
Q3Obtain all other zeroes of 3x4 + 6x3 − 2x2 − 10x − 5, if two of its zeroes are √(5/3) and −√(5/3).▼

Method: If α and −α are zeroes, then (x−α)(x+α) = x²−α² is a factor. Divide p(x) by this factor to find the other zeroes.

Given zeroes: √(5/3) and −√(5/3) These give factor: (x − √(5/3))(x + √(5/3)) = x² − 5/3 Multiply by 3: factor is 3x² − 5 Divide 3x&sup4; + 6x³ − 2x² − 10x − 5 by (3x² − 5): 3x&sup4; ÷ 3x² = x² x²(3x²−5) = 3x&sup4;−5x² Remainder: 6x³+3x²−10x−5 6x³ ÷ 3x² = 2x 2x(3x²−5) = 6x³−10x Remainder: 3x²−5 3x² ÷ 3x² = 1 1(3x²−5) = 3x²−5 Remainder: 0 So: 3x&sup4;+6x³−2x²−10x−5 = (3x²−5)(x²+2x+1) = (3x²−5)(x+1)² Other zeroes from (x+1)² = 0: x = −1 (repeated)
✅Other zeroes are x = −1 and x = −1 (i.e., −1 is a repeated zero).
🎯 Strategy for Finding Remaining Zeroes
  • If two zeroes α and −α are given → factor is x²−α²
  • If a zero α is given → factor is (x−α)
  • Divide p(x) by known factor → quotient gives the other zeroes
  • Factorise the quotient to find remaining zeroes
Q4On dividing x3 − 3x2 + x + 2 by a polynomial g(x), the quotient and remainder were x − 2 and −2x + 4 respectively. Find g(x).▼

Method: Use Division Algorithm: p(x) = g(x) × q(x) + r(x). Rearrange to find g(x) = [p(x) − r(x)] / q(x).

Given: p(x) = x³ − 3x² + x + 2 q(x) = x − 2 r(x) = −2x + 4 From Division Algorithm: p(x) = g(x) × q(x) + r(x) So: g(x) = [p(x) − r(x)] / q(x) p(x) − r(x) = (x³−3x²+x+2) − (−2x+4) = x³ − 3x² + x + 2 + 2x − 4 = x³ − 3x² + 3x − 2 Now divide (x³−3x²+3x−2) by (x−2): x³ ÷ x = x² x²(x−2) = x³−2x² Remainder: −x²+3x−2 −x² ÷ x = −x −x(x−2) = −x²+2x Remainder: x−2 x ÷ x = 1 1(x−2) = x−2 Remainder: 0 g(x) = x² − x + 1
✅g(x) = x² − x + 1

Verify: (x²−x+1)(x−2) + (−2x+4) = x³−2x²−x²+2x+x−2−2x+4 = x³−3x²+x+2 = p(x) ✓

🎯 Key Approach for Finding g(x)
  • Rearrange Division Algorithm: g(x) = [p(x) − r(x)] / q(x)
  • Subtract remainder from dividend first, then divide by quotient
  • Always verify your g(x) by multiplying back: g(x)×q(x)+r(x) should equal p(x)
Q5Give examples of polynomials p(x), g(x), q(x) and r(x), which satisfy the division algorithm and
(i) deg p(x) = deg q(x)    (ii) deg q(x) = deg r(x)    (iii) deg r(x) = 0▼

Method: Construct examples satisfying p(x) = g(x)×q(x)+r(x) for each condition. Use simple polynomials.

(i) deg p(x) = deg q(x)
For deg p(x) = deg q(x), the divisor g(x) must be a constant (degree 0). Example: p(x) = 2x² − 2x + 14 g(x) = 2 q(x) = x² − x + 7 r(x) = 0 Verify: g(x)×q(x) + r(x) = 2(x²−x+7) + 0 = 2x²−2x+14 = p(x) ✓ deg p(x) = 2 = deg q(x) ✓
✅p(x)=2x²−2x+14, g(x)=2, q(x)=x²−x+7, r(x)=0
(ii) deg q(x) = deg r(x)
Example: p(x) = x³ + x² + x + 1 g(x) = x² − 1 q(x) = x + 1 r(x) = 2x + 2 Verify: (x²−1)(x+1) + (2x+2) = x³+x²−x−1+2x+2 = x³+x²+x+1 ✓ deg q(x) = 1 = deg r(x) ✓
✅p(x)=x³+x²+x+1, g(x)=x²−1, q(x)=x+1, r(x)=2x+2
(iii) deg r(x) = 0
deg r(x) = 0 means remainder is a non-zero constant. Example: p(x) = x³ + 2x² − x + 2 g(x) = x² − 1 q(x) = x + 2 r(x) = 4 (constant) Verify: (x²−1)(x+2) + 4 = x³+2x²−x−2+4 = x³+2x²−x+2 ✓ deg r(x) = 0 ✓
✅p(x)=x³+2x²−x+2, g(x)=x²−1, q(x)=x+2, r(x)=4
🎯 Important Conditions for Division Algorithm
  • Always: degree of r(x) < degree of g(x), OR r(x) = 0
  • deg q(x) = deg p(x) − deg g(x) (when division is exact or near-exact)
  • For deg p = deg q: g(x) must be a constant (degree 0)
  • Always verify your example by computing g(x)×q(x)+r(x) and checking it equals p(x)
Telangana State Board · Chapter 3 · Polynomials

Chapter 3 — Master Cheat Sheet

All key definitions, formulas, rules and exam facts for Chapter 3. Revise before your exam!

Quick Revision All 4 Exercises

Master Cheat Sheet — Chapter 3: Polynomials

BASICS (EX 3.1)

  • Degree = highest power of variable
  • Coefficient includes the − sign!
  • Constant term = number with no variable
  • p(k) = value when x is replaced by k
  • Zero: k is a zero if p(k) = 0
  • NOT polynomials: 1/x, √x, x−1

GRAPH & ZEROES (EX 3.2)

  • Zeroes = x-coordinates of X-axis crossings
  • Degree n → at most n zeroes
  • Parabola crosses X-axis: 2 distinct zeroes
  • Parabola touches X-axis: 1 repeated zero
  • Parabola above/below X-axis: no real zeroes
  • 3 cases depend on discriminant b²−4ac

ZEROES & COEFFICIENTS (EX 3.3)

  • Sum of zeroes α+β = −b/a
  • Product of zeroes αβ = c/a
  • Form quadratic: x²−(sum)x+(product)
  • Cubic: α+β+γ = −b/a
  • Cubic: αβ+βγ+γα = c/a
  • Cubic: αβγ = −d/a

DIVISION ALGORITHM (EX 3.4)

  • p(x) = g(x)×q(x) + r(x)
  • deg r(x) < deg g(x) always
  • Standard form: write decreasing powers
  • Factor test: remainder = 0 ⇔ g(x) is factor
  • Always verify by expanding g×q+r = p
  • For remaining zeroes: divide by known factor
